target.title: fix(gateway): render codex inline media once
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Codex-harness replies that emitted local `MEDIA:` image directives could fail to become trusted outgoing media, leaving inline images text-only or unavailable to WebChat/Control UI.
- Why it matters: Agents using the Codex harness should be able to return generated/local images inline without requiring channel-specific manual workarounds, and users should not see duplicate assistant turns for one image reply.
- What changed: Trust structured local media markers from non-external tool results, while preserving the existing registered-tool trust checks; replace the latest raw assistant `MEDIA:` transcript turn with the normalized media-bearing message instead of appending a second assistant image turn.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): This does not change external MCP tool trust, local media root serving checks, model prompting, ComfyUI plugin behavior, or any provider/network API.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

No matching upstream issue was found. Searches checked: `inline image`, `inline media`, `Codex harness image`, `MEDIA: image`, `assistant-media`, `managed media`, `Control UI image`, and duplicate image/WebChat terms.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: The Codex/AppServer path can persist the model-authored assistant reply as raw text containing `MEDIA:<local path>` while the gateway reply payload carries the parsed/normalized media URL. The WebChat transcript bridge treated those as separate assistant replies and appended the normalized media message after the raw directive turn.
- Missing detection / guardrail: Existing media tests covered parsing and directive stripping, but did not cover the Codex/AppServer split where the raw transcript text and normalized media payload represent the same reply.
- Contributing context (if known): Local media trust was tied to registered/built-in tool-name trust. Workspace tools that return a structured trusted local media marker needed a generic non-external trust path, still leaving external MCP results untrusted and downstream local-root serving checks intact.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file:
  - `src/agents/pi-embedded-subscribe.tools.media.test.ts`
  - `src/gateway/server-methods/chat.directive-tags.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in:
  - Non-external structured `trustedLocalMedia` tool results keep local media URLs, while external tool results with the same marker do not.
  - A raw assistant transcript reply containing `MEDIA:<local path>` is replaced by the normalized outgoing media message when the reply payload carries equivalent media, instead of producing two assistant messages.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: These tests hit the two affected seams directly: tool-result media trust filtering and WebChat transcript persistence/replacement.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): Existing directive/media tests covered stripping `MEDIA:` directives into outgoing media blocks, but not replacement of a raw persisted Codex/AppServer assistant turn.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A; new regression coverage is included.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Codex-harness agents can render trusted local image replies inline in WebChat/Control UI without leaving a raw `MEDIA:` line behind or showing a duplicate text-plus-image assistant reply.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[Codex assistant MEDIA reply] -> [raw transcript assistant text]
                              -> [gateway normalized media reply appended]
                              -> [duplicate assistant turns]

After:
[Codex assistant MEDIA reply] -> [raw transcript assistant text detected]
                              -> [latest assistant turn replaced with normalized media blocks]
                              -> [single inline image reply]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`): No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`): No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

Security notes:

- Structured local media trust is ignored for external MCP tool results.
- Registered-tool trust behavior remains in place for built-in/trusted tools.
- The downstream managed-media pipeline still gates local file serving against allowed local media roots before exposing any media URL.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux local development workspace; Windows gateway hot-patch was separately smoke-tested before PR preparation.
- Runtime/container: Node local workspace.
- Model/provider: Codex harness path observed with `gpt-5.5` during manual smoke testing.
- Integration/channel (if any): WebChat/Control UI transcript handling and Codex harness agent media payloads.
- Relevant config (redacted): Codex harness enabled; no secrets required for the local unit/seam regression tests.

### Steps

1. Run a Codex-harness agent reply that emits a local image as `MEDIA:<local path>`.
2. Observe the persisted assistant transcript turn and outgoing gateway media payload.
3. Open WebChat/Control UI for the same session.

### Expected

- The reply appears once as an inline image-bearing assistant message.
- The raw `MEDIA:` directive is not shown to the user.
- Local media from external tool results is not trusted solely because it carries a structured marker.

### Actual

- Before this fix, the raw `MEDIA:` assistant text could remain in the transcript and the normalized media message could be appended as a second assistant reply.
- Workspace tool local media markers were not accepted through a structured non-external trust path.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

Passing local verification on the rebased branch:

```text
node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.core.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core.tsbuildinfo
# passed

node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.core.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core-test.tsbuildinfo
# passed

node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/agents/pi-embedded-subscribe.tools.media.test.ts
# 42 passed

OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_PROJECT_SHARDS=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.gateway.config.ts src/gateway/server-methods/chat.directive-tags.test.ts
# 132 passed
```

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios:

Current head tested: 2ce02670381be9035fc3cb98ae89118d37b29482.

target.title: fix(gateway): report unqueued restart continuations
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: `gateway` restart with `continuationMessage` could return success while coalescing with an already in-flight restart, even though the continuation hook was never queued.
- Why it matters: the agent/user saw a successful restart response but the promised follow-up turn was silently lost.
- What changed: `scheduleGatewaySigusr1Restart` now reports whether emit hooks were queued, and the gateway tool reports `continuationQueued` when a continuation was requested.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): this PR does not attempt to merge a new continuation into an already emitted restart; it makes the non-delivery explicit so callers can react.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #74424
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: in-flight restart coalescing reports that continuation hooks were not queued.
- Real environment tested: macOS 26.5, Node v22.22.0, pnpm 11.1.0, source checkout at `upstream/main` plus this one commit.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```sh
node --import tsx -e 'import { __testing, emitGatewayRestart, scheduleGatewaySigusr1Restart } from "./src/infra/restart.ts"; __testing.resetSigusr1State(); let beforeEmitCalls=0; const handler=()=>{}; process.on("SIGUSR1", handler); try { const emitted=emitGatewayRestart("manual-proof"); const result=scheduleGatewaySigusr1Restart({delayMs:0,reason:"manual-proof-2",emitHooks:{beforeEmit:async()=>{ beforeEmitCalls+=1; }}}); console.log(JSON.stringify({emitted,coalesced:result.coalesced,emitHooksQueued:result.emitHooksQueued,beforeEmitCalls,continuationQueuedWouldBe:result.emitHooksQueued}, null, 2)); } finally { process.removeListener("SIGUSR1", handler); __testing.resetSigusr1State(); }'
```

- Evidence after fix (terminal capture):

```text
[restart] request coalesced (already in-flight) reason=manual-proof-2 actor=<unknown>
{
  "emitted": true,
  "coalesced": true,
  "emitHooksQueued": false,
  "beforeEmitCalls": 0,
  "continuationQueuedWouldBe": false
}
```

- Observed result after fix: the scheduler reports `emitHooksQueued: false` in the already-emitted coalescing path, so the gateway tool can report `continuationQueued: false`.
- What was not tested: a full live gateway restart cycle with a real user-visible post-restart continuation; the in-process proof exercises the exact scheduler branch that previously accepted hooks without running or retaining them.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): issue #74424 includes the observed successful/coalesced restart response with no follow-up turn.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: `scheduleGatewaySigusr1Restart` returned early for `hasUnconsumedRestartSignal()` before registering or running the caller's `emitHooks`, while the gateway tool returned the scheduler success shape without saying the continuation was not queued.
- Missing detection / guardrail: no regression covered the already-emitted coalescing branch with restart continuation hooks.
- Contributing context (if known): config/plugin workflows can schedule restarts while an agent also calls `gateway.restart` with `continuationMessage`.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file:
  - `src/infra/infra-runtime.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/tools/gateway-tool.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in:
  - scheduled coalescing with hooks reports `emitHooksQueued: true`
  - already-emitted coalescing with hooks reports `emitHooksQueued: false`
  - gateway restart result includes `continuationQueued: false` and does not write a sentinel when hooks were not queued
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the dropped continuation happened in the restart scheduler/tool seam, before any post-restart delivery code could run.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): none before this PR.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

`gateway.restart` results with a requested continuation can now include `continuationQueued`. In already in-flight coalescing cases this is `false` instead of silently implying the continuation was queued.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
restart already emitted -> gateway restart with continuation -> coalesced ok -> continuation silently lost

After:
restart already emitted -> gateway restart with continuation -> coalesced ok + continuationQueued=false
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

target.title: fix(gateway): preserve raw external session aliases
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: raw structured external session keys such as `conversation:pair:user_a::user_b:space:space_123` could be pruned as legacy aliases after gateway agent canonicalization.
- Why it matters: the next inbound message for the same raw external key could miss the existing session entry and create a fresh `sessionId`, splitting one logical conversation.
- What changed: `migrateAndPruneGatewaySessionStoreKey` now preserves non-agent raw structured external aliases and mirrors the canonical entry back to that raw key.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): this does not change agent-scoped key canonicalization, reset/rollover behavior, or migrate all historical store keys.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #63005
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: raw external key remains usable after canonical gateway session migration/pruning.
- Real environment tested: macOS 26.5, Node v22.22.0, pnpm 11.1.0, source checkout at `upstream/main` plus this one commit.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```sh
node --import tsx -e 'import { migrateAndPruneGatewaySessionStoreKey } from "./src/gateway/session-utils.ts"; const raw="conversation:pair:user_a::user_b:space:space_123"; const cfg={session:{mainKey:"main"},agents:{list:[{id:"main",default:true}]}}; const store={ [raw]: { sessionId:"sess-raw", updatedAt:2 } }; const result=migrateAndPruneGatewaySessionStoreKey({cfg,key:raw,store}); console.log(JSON.stringify({primaryKey:result.primaryKey,resultSessionId:result.entry?.sessionId,rawSessionId:store[raw]?.sessionId,canonicalSessionId:store[result.primaryKey]?.sessionId,keys:Object.keys(store).sort()}, null, 2));'
```

- Evidence after fix (terminal capture):

```json
{
  "primaryKey": "agent:main:conversation:pair:user_a::user_b:space:space_123",
  "resultSessionId": "sess-raw",
  "rawSessionId": "sess-raw",
  "canonicalSessionId": "sess-raw",
  "keys": [
    "agent:main:conversation:pair:user_a::user_b:space:space_123",
    "conversation:pair:user_a::user_b:space:space_123"
  ]
}
```

- Observed result after fix: both the canonical key and raw external key point at the same `sessionId`.
- What was not tested: a live third-party external integration; this uses the sanitized key shape from the issue against the real session-store migration helper.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): issue #63005 documents the old split shape and the pruned raw-key path.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Contributing context (if known): inbound/session-init paths may still receive and look up the raw external key.

  - [ ] End-to-end test

- Scenario the test should lock in: migrating `conversation:pair:user_a::user_b:space:space_123` preserves both raw and canonical store keys with the same session entry.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the split came from alias pruning in the session-store helper, so the deterministic helper test catches it without channel credentials.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): none before this PR.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Existing external conversations that rely on stable structured raw keys are less likely to split after internal gateway/nested-agent activity.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
raw external key -> canonical gateway key -> raw alias pruned -> later raw inbound can start a new session

After:

```

- New/changed network calls? No

target.title: fix(outbound): log failDelivery rejection on bestEffort partial failure (#83113)
target.body:
Closes #83113

## Problem

In `src/infra/outbound/deliver.ts` (around line 1298), when best-effort partial delivery occurs the queue entry is marked failed via `failDelivery()` so the retry count is incremented. The previous code wrapped that call in `.catch(() => {})`, silently swallowing any rejection from `failDelivery` itself.

If `failDelivery` rejects (e.g. queue storage write fails), the entry's retry count is never incremented, recovery may retry the un-acked entry, and the operator gets no journal-facing hint that anything went wrong. This is inconsistent with the surrounding `ackDelivery` path (~lines 1305-1316) which logs warnings via `log.warn` + `formatErrorMessage`.

## Fix

Replace the silent `.catch(() => {})` with a `log.warn` that includes:

- the queue id
- a hint that the queue entry may be retried
- `formatErrorMessage(err)` for the underlying cause

This matches the existing `ackDelivery` warning style. The non-required best-effort branch keeps continuing (no rethrow) so behavior on success paths is unchanged — only the silent-failure observability gap is closed.

## Test

Added regression test `logs a warning when failDelivery itself rejects on bestEffort partial failure` in `src/infra/outbound/deliver.test.ts` that mocks `failDelivery` to reject and asserts the warn line contains the queue id and underlying error message.

```
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/infra/outbound/deliver.test.ts
# 83 tests passed (1 new)
```

Lint clean (`oxlint`), no other call sites touched. Narrowly scoped to the specifically-flagged path; the other `failDelivery(...).catch(() => {})` on line ~1328 is a different control flow (the outer catch already has a logged error to work from) and is intentionally left untouched to keep the PR small.

## Found by

Codebase audit (finding F019), reported in #83113.

target.title: feat(telegram): bind spawned subagents to forum topics
target.body:
## Summary

- Adds an explicit `supportsAutomaticThreadBindingSpawn` channel capability so automatic thread-bound session spawn support is not overloaded onto `defaultTopLevelPlacement`.
- Wires Telegram `subagent_spawning`, `subagent_delivery_target`, and `subagent_ended` hooks so `sessions_spawn(thread:true)` for subagents can create and bind a child forum topic while keeping Telegram's normal top-level binding placement as `current`.
- Makes the spawn capability per-kind (`subagent` vs `acp`) so Telegram advertises subagent forum-topic spawn support without incorrectly exposing ACP child-topic spawn.
- Documents the new capability seam and adds Telegram/core regression coverage for schema exposure, runtime capabilities, hook registration, binding, completion routing, cleanup, and boundary invariants.

## Context

Telegram already had forum-topic primitives and session binding infrastructure, but it lacked the subagent lifecycle hook wiring and an explicit capability to say: "explicit subagent thread spawns can create child topics, while ordinary top-level binding should stay in the current conversation." Discord and Matrix naturally expose automatic thread spawns through `defaultTopLevelPlacement: "child"`; Feishu keeps `current` placement and does not advertise child-thread session spawn. Telegram now follows the narrower model: keep normal Telegram `/focus` and top-level binding behavior current, while allowing explicit `sessions_spawn(thread:true)` for subagents to create a child forum topic when the Telegram adapter can do so.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: Telegram `sessions_spawn(thread:true, mode:"session")` from a channel-bound CodeClaw triage session creates and binds a real Telegram forum topic for the spawned subagent session, so follow-up work routes to that topic instead of becoming invisible or falling back to the parent topic.
- Real environment tested: Live `codeclaw-triage` Docker container after installing a custom OpenClaw package built from combined proof branch `codeclaw-proof-82023-82275` (https://github.com/efpiva/openclaw/tree/codeclaw-proof-82023-82275) at `OpenClaw 2026.5.17 (db1c53a)`. The container was connected to the real CodeClaw Telegram forum supergroup and used the real CodeClaw triage cron job.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

  ```bash
  docker exec --user codeclaw codeclaw-triage     HOME=/home/codeclaw openclaw cron run cc373f35-b483-4c7f-9253-2f6c9c94a595       --wait --wait-timeout 15m --timeout 30000
  ```

- Evidence after fix:

  The triage transcript for run `1d6d77dd-c23b-4b02-9831-e990fb11a7fc` shows the initial PR action for private CodeClaw PR #1223 was spawned with `thread:true` and `mode:"session"`:

  ```json
  {
    "type": "toolCall",
    "name": "sessions_spawn",
    "arguments": {
      "agentId": "main",
      "label": "codeclaw-pr:external:microsoft.ghe.com:bic/lobster:1223",
      "mode": "session",
      "context": "isolated",
      "thread": true
    }
  }
  ```

  The tool result accepted the spawn and returned a thread-bound child session:

  ```json
  {
    "toolName": "sessions_spawn",
    "isError": false,
    "details": {
      "status": "accepted",
      "childSessionKey": "agent:main:subagent:c89515e3-1a1e-4465-8fe9-8b25e85c7819",
      "runId": "23538111-3540-4645-a66b-cc0568f96de0",
      "mode": "session",
      "note": "thread-bound session stays active after this task; continue in-thread for follow-ups.",
      "modelApplied": true
    }
  }
  ```

  Live Telegram/OpenClaw logs show the forum topic was created and bound to that child session. The Telegram supergroup id is redacted, but the topic id and session key are unchanged:

  ```text
  2026-05-17T15:07:57.282-07:00 telegram: bound conversation <telegram-supergroup-id>:topic:1167 -> agent:main:subagent:c89515e3-1a1e-4465-8fe9-8b25e85c7819 (idle=24h maxAge=disabled)
  2026-05-17T15:07:57.406-07:00 [telegram] update: {"message":{"message_id":1167,"chat":{"title":"CodeClaw Activity","is_forum":true,"type":"supergroup"},"message_thread_id":1167,"forum_topic_created":{"name":"🤖 codeclaw-pr:external:microsoft.ghe.com:bic/lobster:1223"},"is_topic_message":true}}
  2026-05-17T15:07:57.923-07:00 [agent/embedded] embedded run start: runId=23538111-3540-4645-a66b-cc0568f96de0 sessionId=ada5b4d0-65b5-48ff-8c81-76c2ab477ab8 provider=github-copilot model=gpt-5.5 thinking=medium messageChannel=telegram
  2026-05-17T15:07:58.444-07:00 [agent/embedded] [context-diag] pre-prompt: sessionKey=agent:main:subagent:c89515e3-1a1e-4465-8fe9-8b25e85c7819 ... sessionFile=/home/codeclaw/.openclaw/agents/main/sessions/ada5b4d0-65b5-48ff-8c81-76c2ab477ab8-topic-1167.jsonl
  ```

  The persisted Telegram binding file contains the same mapping:

  ```json
  {
    "conversationId": "<telegram-supergroup-id>:topic:1167",
    "targetKind": "subagent",
    "targetSessionKey": "agent:main:subagent:c89515e3-1a1e-4465-8fe9-8b25e85c7819",
    "agentId": "main",
    "label": "codeclaw-pr:external:microsoft.ghe.com:bic/lobster:1223",
    "metadata": {
      "threadName": "🤖 codeclaw-pr:external:microsoft.ghe.com:bic/lobster:1223"
    }
  }
  ```

  The CodeClaw poller state also persisted the spawned native session key for that PR:

  ```json
  {
    "source": "pr_reviews",
    "id": "microsoft.ghe.com|bic/lobster#1223",
    "lastKind": "initial",
    "nativeSessionKey": "agent:main:subagent:c89515e3-1a1e-4465-8fe9-8b25e85c7819"
  }
  ```

- Observed result after fix: The live Telegram Bot API created forum topic `1167`, OpenClaw bound it to the spawned `main` subagent session, the child session ran with `messageChannel=telegram`, and all child/specialist session transcript files for the spawned review used the `topic-1167` suffix. CodeClaw recorded `nativeSessionKey` for future follow-ups.
- What was not tested: Telegram permission-denied and non-forum failure paths in the live container; those remain covered by local mocked tests. The live proof used one real forum supergroup and one real CodeClaw triage run.

## Supplemental validation


Behavior addressed: Telegram subagent `sessions_spawn(thread:true)` now has an explicit per-kind automatic-spawn capability and lifecycle hooks that bind spawned subagents to child forum topics without enabling Telegram ACP child-topic spawns.

Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw source checkout with mocked Telegram forum topic creation and session binding adapters; no live Telegram Bot API/forum supergroup was used.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs \
  extensions/telegram/src/subagent-hooks.test.ts \
  extensions/telegram/index.test.ts \
  extensions/telegram/src/thread-bindings.test.ts \
  src/channels/plugins/thread-binding-api.test.ts \
  src/channels/thread-bindings-policy.test.ts \
  src/agents/runtime-capabilities.test.ts \
  src/agents/tools/sessions-spawn-tool.test.ts \
  src/agents/acp-spawn.test.ts \
  src/plugins/contracts/boundary-invariants.test.ts
pnpm docs:check-mdx docs/plugins/sdk-channel-plugins.md
git diff --check
pnpm build
codex review --uncommitted
```

Evidence after fix: Targeted Vitest proof passed with 12 files and 245 tests; docs MDX check passed; whitespace check passed; `pnpm build` completed including plugin SDK export verification; final Codex CLI review reported no actionable correctness issues.

Observed result after fix: Telegram advertises `thread` for subagent session spawn through the new explicit per-kind capability, returns a routable Telegram delivery origin for created forum topics, routes completion to the bound topic, cleans up Telegram subagent bindings, and does not advertise/recommend ACP thread-bound spawns when ACP support is disabled for Telegram.

What was not tested: Live Telegram Bot API behavior in a real forum supergroup with `can_manage_topics`; non-forum group and permission failure behavior were covered only through local unit paths/mocked failures.

target.title: Route allow-always through command authorization planner
target.body:
## Summary
- Add the command authorization planner contract and Tree-sitter-backed POSIX planner corpus.
- Route POSIX allowlist evaluation, allow-always persistence, approval display summaries, control-command rejection, and enforced shell rendering through the planner.
- Remove the legacy POSIX chain/pipeline/heredoc parser from exec approval analysis; the remaining shell analyzer path is Windows-only.
- Preserve first-miss chain behavior, safe wrapper payload handling, positional carrier guards, pipe-to-shell prompting, and strict inline-eval no-persistence behavior.

## Real Behavior Proof
- Ran a live terminal proof against this branch, outside the unit harness.
- Command: `fnm exec --using 22.21.0 pnpm exec tsx -e '<planner allowlist proof>'`
- Result: planner-rendered enforced pipeline `'/usr/bin/printf' 'hi' | '/usr/bin/wc' '-c'` executed through `/bin/sh -lc` and returned `2`.
- Result: allow-always on `sh -c 'echo ok'` persisted `/bin/echo`; a later `sh -c 'id > /tmp/openclaw-parser-proof-marker'` still had `allowlistSatisfied: false`.

## Verification
- `fnm exec --using 22.21.0 pnpm test src/infra/command-authorization/corpus.test.ts src/infra/exec-approvals-analysis.test.ts src/infra/exec-approvals-allow-always.test.ts src/infra/exec-approvals-store.test.ts src/infra/exec-approvals-safe-bins.test.ts src/infra/command-analysis/explain.test.ts src/infra/command-analysis/explain.lazy.test.ts src/infra/exec-approvals-parity.test.ts src/agents/bash-tools.exec-host-gateway.test.ts src/node-host/invoke-system-run.test.ts`

The PR adds an opt-in Anthropic `claude-cli-interactive` backend that runs Claude Code through a Bun wrapper and loopback TLS proxy to reconstruct Claude stream-json output from intercepted SSE events.

Reproducibility: not applicable. as a current-main bug; this is a new backend feature. The blocking wiring issue is source-reproducible from PR head plus current main: runtime aliasing only recognizes `claude-cli`, and forward-compatible model cloning only preserves that provider.

target.title: feat(anthropic): claude-cli-interactive backend — stream reasoning via local TLS proxy (experimental)
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem.** OpenClaw's `claude-cli` backend invokes `claude -p --output-format stream-json` (headless). Starting **June 15, 2026** that path moves onto Anthropic's separate programmatic credit pool ([VentureBeat coverage](https://venturebeat.com/technology/anthropic-reinstates-openclaw-and-third-party-agent-usage-on-claude-subscriptions-with-a-catch)) — fixed monthly allowance, billed at API rates, doesn't roll over.
- **Why it matters.** Every OpenClaw Opus turn on a Claude subscription will drain that fixed credit post–June 15 instead of counting against the interactive subscription limits subscribers are paying for. Opus turns exhaust the credit fast.
- **What changed.** New opt-in `claude-cli-interactive` backend. Each turn spawns one fresh `bun wrapper.ts …` that boots a loopback HTTPS MITM on a 127.0.0.1 port, taps every API SSE event, re-emits the events as `claude -p --output-format stream-json` JSONL records, and kills the claude child on `end_turn`. A `tty-spoof.cjs` `NODE_OPTIONS=--require` preload keeps claude in interactive mode under piped stdio.
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary).** Existing `claude-cli` backend untouched. `agents.defaults.cliBackends.claude-cli` user overrides inherit into the interactive backend via `inheritUserConfigFrom` with `-p`-mode flags stripped. System CA store is not mutated (`NODE_EXTRA_CA_CERTS` scoped to the spawned claude child only). Non–`api.anthropic.com` CONNECT tunnels pass through unmodified. Tool-use rendering / waiting-on-tool injection lands in #82285, not here.
- **AI-assisted:** yes — drafted with Claude.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed:** `claude` running under OpenClaw in interactive (subscription) mode with full `init` / `stream_event*` / `result` JSONL reconstruction on the wrapper's stdout, sourced from the loopback MITM proxy's SSE tap.
- **Real environment tested:** Windows 11 Pro 26200, Node v24.12.0, pnpm, Bun, `@anthropic-ai/claude-code` v2.1.142 (npm-installed), Opus 4.7 (`claude-opus-4-7`), live OpenClaw gateway driving the backend per-turn over Telegram.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** `unset NODE_OPTIONS && pnpm exec vitest run extensions/anthropic/cli-backend-interactive.test.ts`; `pnpm exec tsc --noEmit -p extensions/anthropic/tsconfig.json`; set `cliBackends.claude-cli-interactive` active in user config; restart the OpenClaw gateway; send a Telegram message that triggers an Opus 4.7 turn; observe wrapper boot in stderr, SSE flow through `127.0.0.1:<port>`, JSONL arriving at OpenClaw; send a follow-up message with `--resume <sessionId>` to verify resume.
- **Evidence after fix:**
  - vitest: `Test Files 1 passed (1) / Tests 5 passed (5)` — `normalizeConfig` branches (inherited claude-cli match, direct Bun-path override, env passthrough, no-override skip).
  - Gateway log excerpt + cert listing + active-config snapshot on the [`proof-assets/pr-81851`](https://github.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/tree/proof-assets/pr-81851/.proof) branch. Embedded sample (one of three real turns in the gateway-log excerpt):

    ```
    cli exec: provider=claude-cli-interactive model=opus useResume=true session=present
    cli argv: bun <NPM>\…\wrapper.ts --effort max --verbose --setting-sources user --resume <SESSION> --allowedTools mcp__openclaw__* --model opus <prompt:N chars>
    [openclaw-proxy] certs ready: <HOME>\.openclaw\proxy-certs\ca.crt
    [openclaw-proxy] MITM proxy active on 127.0.0.1:17081 — Anthropic API traffic intercepted locally to capture thinking_delta events
    [openclaw-proxy] spawning claude binary: <NPM>\…\@anthropic-ai\claude-code\bin\claude.exe
    [openclaw-proxy] result flushed, waiting for session write before kill
    [openclaw-proxy] session file updated, killing claude
    ```
- **Observed result after fix:** per-turn lifecycle terminates cleanly — wrapper emits `init`, a continuous stream of `stream_event` records, then a single `result` on `end_turn`, sets `intentionalKill = true`, and `claude.kill()`s the child so the per-turn process tree exits 0.
- **What was not tested:** Linux/macOS (developed on Windows 11 only); bundled-MCP `claude-config-file` mode end-to-end with multiple MCP servers active (only `mcp__openclaw__*` exercised live); forced Anthropic-side `429` / `overloaded_error` mid-stream (the `api_error` synthetic event path is implemented but I did not provoke a real 429).

## Change Type

- [x] Feature

## Scope

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Integrations

## Linked

- Related: #81550 (programmatic-mode reasoning bridge — this PR is the interactive-mode equivalent on a separate backend); #82285 (tool-use injection, builds on the `stream_event` surface this PR establishes).

## Root Cause

N/A — feature PR. The lack of thinking visibility on subscription is a property of `claude -p` (headless) mode, not a regression in OpenClaw.

## Regression Test Plan

- **Target:** `extensions/anthropic/cli-backend-interactive.test.ts`.
- **Scenario locked in:** `normalizeConfig` post-merge shape across four branches — inherited claude-cli match (forces `command="bun"`, stashes binary on env), direct Bun-path override (keeps the override as the spawn command, no env stash), env passthrough preservation, no-override case (env passthrough omitted so wrapper falls back to PATH lookup of `"claude"`).
- **Why smallest reliable guardrail:** the failure mode is config-shape drift inside `normalizeConfig` — fully exercised synchronously without spawning bun or claude, runs in ms vs the minutes an e2e run would cost.

## Risks and Mitigations

- **Risk:** newly-installed user with no inherited `claude-cli` override could spawn `bun` as the claude binary if `normalizeConfig` stashed `merged.command` indiscriminately.
  - **Mitigation:** `normalizeConfig` consults `context.config.agents.defaults.cliBackends.claude-cli.command`; only stashes the env passthrough on a match. Regression test asserts the env var is `undefined` for the no-override branch.
- **Risk:** `tty-spoof.cjs` is a runtime support file (not a build output) and could be omitted from packaged installs.
  - **Mitigation:** declared in `extensions/anthropic/package.json#openclaw.build.staticAssets`; postbuild asset copier ships it alongside the wrapper.
- **Risk:** winget-installed `claude.exe` shadows the npm install via PATHEXT on Windows.
  - **Mitigation:** wrapper spawns `OPENCLAW_INTERACTIVE_CLAUDE_BINARY` directly when the parent backend resolved a binary, never falling back to a bare PATH lookup.
- **Risk:** title-gen / Haiku side-requests inject `{"title":"…"}` artifacts into the reasoning stream.
  - **Mitigation:** mitm-server classifies each `/v1/messages` request into `auxiliary` (no tools) / `compaction` (compact.ts prompt markers) / `tool_followup` (last msg has tool_result) / `normal`. Wrapper drops auxiliary entirely, streams compaction as `thinking_delta`, emits a result only on `normal` `end_turn`. Response-content fingerprint backup (`<analysis>` + Primary-Request-and-Intent + Pending-Tasks) catches anything that slipped past the request-level classifier.
- **Risk:** spilled-prompt overflow file leaks to concurrent runs via shared tempdir + `--add-dir`.
  - **Mitigation:** per-run subdir under `${tmpdir()}/openclaw-interactive-proxy/run-<32-hex-bytes>/` (0700), file 0600, `--add-dir` scoped to the per-run subdir only.
- **Risk:** local CA + leaf key persisted under user's OpenClaw config dir could be reused if an attacker gets read access.
  - **Mitigation:** trust scoped to the spawned claude child via `NODE_EXTRA_CA_CERTS` only (no system trust mutation); CA issues solely the one `api.anthropic.com` leaf the per-turn proxy needs; key files hardened to 0600 with fail-closed regeneration; maintainer security review invited.
- **Risk:** Anthropic-side non-SSE 4xx/5xx on `/v1/messages` (429, billing, overloaded) hangs the wrapper because claude doesn't self-exit on API error.
  - **Mitigation:** mitm-server emits a synthetic `api_error` event for non-SSE 4xx/5xx; wrapper surfaces a `result` with `is_error: true` and exits nonzero so the CLI runner treats the turn as failed and triggers failover.
- **Risk:** two resumes of the same Claude session race on the same transcript.
  - **Mitigation:** backend config sets `serialize: true` so the framework queues same-session runs; different sessions still run concurrently. mitm-server binds both proxy stages on port 0 so concurrent proxies can't collide on a preferred port either.

## Security Impact

- **New permissions/capabilities:** Yes — per-turn local TLS MITM on a loopback port. Scoped to the spawned claude child via `NODE_EXTRA_CA_CERTS`; system trust store untouched. Local CA + leaf key cached under user's OpenClaw config dir at 0700/0600.
- **Secrets/tokens handling changed:** No — wrapper does not read, log, or modify the Anthropic auth token; it is forwarded byte-for-byte to the re-issued upstream request.
- **New/changed network calls:** Yes — claude's outbound HTTPS to `api.anthropic.com:443` is rerouted via `HTTPS_PROXY=http://127.0.0.1:<port>` inside the child. Non–`api.anthropic.com` CONNECT tunnels (statsig, datadog, etc.) bypass the TLS terminator entirely.
- **Command/tool execution surface changed:** No — same `claude` binary, same allowed-tools (`mcp__openclaw__*`), same session semantics.
- **Data access scope changed:** No — proxy sees only the SSE bytes claude itself sees on the wire; nothing exfiltrated or aggregated outside the per-turn process.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible: existing `claude-cli` backend unchanged. Opt-in via the `claude-cli-interactive` id; no default flips.
- New optional env: `OPENCLAW_INTERACTIVE_PROXY_DEBUG=1` enables verbose wrapper stderr.
- Migration needed: no.

## Prior art

[`smithersai/claude-p`](https://github.com/smithersai/claude-p) runs `claude` without `-p` but bypasses the JSONL streaming surface entirely — works for the raw final text but loses the `stream_event` records that reasoning and tool-use rendering depend on. This PR reproduces the `-p --output-format stream-json` shape record-for-record from the SSE tap so existing OpenClaw stream consumers layer on unchanged.

target.title: feat(diagnostics): add skill usage telemetry
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: tool execution telemetry existed, but skill usage was not exported as a first-class diagnostics signal.
- Why it matters: operators could see `read`/`exec` tool calls, but could not build Prometheus/Grafana or OTEL dashboards showing which skills were actually used.
- What changed: added trusted `skill.used` diagnostics when a run reads a known `SKILL.md` or completes a command-dispatched skill tool, then exported it via `diagnostics-otel` and `diagnostics-prometheus`.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no skill loading, skill selection, command execution, permissions, auth, or model behavior changed.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: skill usage is now visible as diagnostics telemetry instead of only indirect tool calls.
- Real environment tested: local OpenClaw repo on macOS, Node 22.22.2.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: `pnpm test src/agents/pi-tools.before-tool-call.e2e.test.ts extensions/diagnostics-prometheus/src/service.test.ts extensions/diagnostics-otel/src/service.test.ts`
- Evidence after fix: targeted tests passed: 3 Vitest shards, 3 files, 91 tests total.
- Observed result after fix: core emits `skill.used`; Prometheus renders `openclaw_skill_used_total`; OTEL records `openclaw.skill.used` counter/span.
- What was not tested: live Gateway + Grafana dashboard scrape.
- Before evidence: N/A; this is a feature addition, not a regression fix.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: N/A.
- Missing detection / guardrail: N/A.
- Contributing context (if known): tool telemetry counted tool calls, but skills are prompt/read based and did not previously have their own diagnostic event.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

N/A for bug regression.

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [ ] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [x] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/agents/pi-tools.before-tool-call.e2e.test.ts`, `extensions/diagnostics-prometheus/src/service.test.ts`, `extensions/diagnostics-otel/src/service.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: known skill file reads and command-dispatched skill tools emit/export bounded skill telemetry.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: it covers the core diagnostic emission point and both telemetry exporters.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): newly added targeted tests.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Adds new diagnostics output:

- Prometheus: `openclaw_skill_used_total{activation,agent,skill,source}`
- OTEL: `openclaw.skill.used` counter and `openclaw.skill.used` span

No config changes required.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
agent reads SKILL.md -> read tool telemetry only -> no skill usage dashboard signal

After:
agent reads SKILL.md -> skill.used diagnostic -> OTEL/Prometheus export -> dashboardable skill usage

commands.author: yetval

target.title: fix(followup): stop model-fallback retries duplicating queued user messages and assistant error stubs
target.body:
## Summary

- Followup queue + model fallback: latch queued user-message persistence so only the first fallback candidate writes the queued user message to the session JSONL.
- Followup queue + model fallback: suppress the `[assistant turn failed before producing content]` stub on every fallback candidate after the first, so the JSONL never gains consecutive assistant-error entries.
- Add `suppressAssistantErrorPersistence` to the session tool-result guard and wrapper; plumb through pi-embedded-runner params, the embedded attempt, and the run-time overload loop.
- Regression tests: contract-level (the runner receives the right suppress flags on each fallback candidate) and behavior-level (real `SessionManager` + guard cascade ends with exactly `[user, assistant]` and zero consecutive same-role pairs).

## Why

With `messages.queue.mode: "followup"`, `runWithModelFallback` invokes its run callback once per candidate model. Each invocation re-passed `queued.transcriptPrompt` into the embedded runner, the session manager re-appended the queued user message, and every failed candidate wrote an `[assistant turn failed before producing content]` stub. The session ended up with consecutive same-role entries (4x user in followup mode, 4x assistant in steer mode); the next request was rejected because Anthropic does not accept consecutive same-role messages, OpenRouter masked the upstream 400 as a 500, and the failover loop ran again — death spiral, ~4KB session growth per cycle, unrecoverable without manual session deletion.

The fix is two latches in `createFollowupRunner`'s `runWithModelFallback` run callback:

1. `queuedUserMessagePersistedAcrossFallback` — flipped by `onUserMessagePersisted`. ORed into `suppressNextUserMessagePersistence` so the second+ candidate skips re-appending the queued user message.
2. `fallbackCandidateAttemptIndex` — counter; passes `suppressAssistantErrorPersistence: true` on every candidate after the first. The session tool-result guard then skips assistant messages with `stopReason === "error"`.

CLI runner branch is untouched: it does not accept either suppression flag and owns its own transcript management. Scope limited to the embedded path described in the issue.

Closes #83404

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: queued followup user messages and per-candidate assistant error stubs duplicated in the session JSONL on model-fallback retry, producing consecutive same-role entries that the provider then rejects (https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/83404).

Real environment tested: behavior-level integration against the real `SessionManager` (`@earendil-works/pi-coding-agent`) plus `installSessionToolResultGuard` from this branch. A four-candidate failover cascade is driven through the real persistence stack in both `followup` and `steer` queue modes, with the new suppression flags forced off versus on, and the resulting session journal is read back and inspected. The terminal output below is the captured stdout of that integration cascade.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

    node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/session-tool-result-guard.test.ts \
      -t "four-candidate followup fallback cascade"
    node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/followup-runner.test.ts \
      -t "queued user message idempotency across fallback"
    node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/followup-runner.test.ts \
      -t "only persists assistant error stub on the first fallback candidate"

Evidence after fix: redacted terminal capture of the session journal contents for each scenario, taken directly from the integration cascade's debug print of the persisted entries:

    mode=followup applyFix=false
      entries=8 role-sequence=[user, assistant, user, assistant, user, assistant, user, assistant]
      consecutive same-role pairs=0
        user: queued user message
        assistant stopReason=error: [assistant turn failed before producing content]
        user: queued user message
        assistant stopReason=error: [assistant turn failed before producing content]
        user: queued user message
        assistant stopReason=error: [assistant turn failed before producing content]
        user: queued user message
        assistant stopReason=error: [assistant turn failed before producing content]

    mode=followup applyFix=true
      entries=2 role-sequence=[user, assistant]
      consecutive same-role pairs=0
        user: queued user message
        assistant stopReason=error: [assistant turn failed before producing content]

    mode=steer applyFix=false
      entries=5 role-sequence=[user, assistant, assistant, assistant, assistant]
      consecutive same-role pairs=3
        user: queued user message
        assistant stopReason=error: [assistant turn failed before producing content]
        assistant stopReason=error: [assistant turn failed before producing content]
        assistant stopReason=error: [assistant turn failed before producing content]
        assistant stopReason=error: [assistant turn failed before producing content]

    mode=steer applyFix=true
      entries=2 role-sequence=[user, assistant]
      consecutive same-role pairs=0
        user: queued user message
        assistant stopReason=error: [assistant turn failed before producing content]

Observed result after fix: session journal collapses from 8 entries (followup) / 5 entries with 3 consecutive same-role pairs (steer) down to exactly 2 entries with zero consecutive same-role pairs in both modes. The exact session-JSONL shape from the issue — `user, user, user, user` in followup and `assistant, assistant, assistant, assistant` in steer — is no longer produced. Subsequent inbound messages will no longer hit the provider's consecutive-same-role validation and the death-spiral re-failover described in the issue cannot start.

What was not tested: a live OpenRouter run against four real anthropic fallback models on a real channel. Reporter's exact configuration was reproduced through the real session-persistence stack but not against a live provider; a maintainer with provider credentials should run the same flow end-to-end on a real account before merge.

## Verification

Build:

    pnpm build

passes (required because `params.ts` and the guard wrapper grew a new public field).

Targeted tests:

    node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/session-tool-result-guard.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/followup-runner.test.ts

Result: `Test Files 2 passed (2) | Tests 76 passed (76)`.

The new `installSessionToolResultGuard > models a four-candidate followup fallback cascade producing exactly one user and one assistant-error entry` test asserts the same invariant the runtime journal above shows, executed inside CI on every push.

target.title: fix: resolve QMD Windows shims and guard image URL downloads
target.body:
## Summary

Fix two Windows / provider-compatibility issues:

1. QMD binary resolution on Windows when `memory.qmd.command` resolves to a `.cmd` shim instead of a native executable.
2. OpenAI-compatible image generation responses that return `data[].url` instead of `data[].b64_json`.

The QMD issue was found with QMD installed via:

```bash
npm install -g @tobilu/qmd
```

In the affected Windows environment, the working `qmd` command was provided by a `.cmd` wrapper like:

```bat
@echo off
node "E:\software\encoding\common\nodeJs\node_global\node_modules\@tobilu\qmd\dist\cli\qmd.js" %*
```

Running this wrapper from the shell worked normally, but OpenClaw's Windows wrapper resolver only handled `%~dp0`-relative shim targets. Quoted absolute JS/EXE entrypoints were skipped, so QMD availability checks failed closed with:

```text
qmd.CMD wrapper resolved, but no executable/Node entrypoint could be resolved without shell execution
```

This PR allows Windows `.cmd` wrappers to resolve quoted absolute JS/EXE entrypoints without enabling shell execution.

It also updates QMD package fallback resolution from `qmd` to the actual npm package name, `@tobilu/qmd`.

For image generation, some OpenAI-compatible providers such as NewAPI return generated images as hosted URLs:

```json
{
  "data": [
    {
      "url": "https://provider.example/generated-image.png"
    }
  ]
}
```

OpenClaw previously only parsed `data[].b64_json`, so these responses produced no generated image assets.

This PR adds support for `data[].url` while routing provider-controlled URL downloads through guarded provider HTTP.

## Changes

### QMD / Windows

- Support quoted absolute Windows entrypoints in `.cmd` shim parsing
- Preserve the existing `%~dp0`-relative shim handling
- Apply the same resolver fix to both:
  - plugin SDK Windows spawn resolver
  - memory host SDK Windows spawn resolver
- Use `@tobilu/qmd` for QMD package fallback resolution
- Add unit coverage for `.cmd` shims that quote an absolute JavaScript entrypoint

### OpenAI-compatible image generation

- Parse OpenAI-compatible image responses that include `data[].url`
- Prefer `data[].b64_json` when both `b64_json` and `url` are present
- Download URL-based image assets and infer MIME/file extension from response headers and image bytes
- Route provider-returned image URL downloads through `fetchWithTimeoutGuarded(...)`
- Preserve provider HTTP guardrails for:
  - timeout
  - SSRF/private-network policy
  - dispatcher/proxy policy
  - audit context
  - guarded response release handling
- Add unit coverage proving URL downloads use guarded provider HTTP instead of raw fetch

## Security

Provider-controlled image URLs are not fetched with raw `fetch`.

The follow-up URL download path uses `fetchWithTimeoutGuarded(...)` and carries the same provider HTTP controls used for image generation requests:

- request timeout
- SSRF policy
- private-network opt-in
- dispatcher/proxy policy
- audit context
- guarded response release handling

This avoids bypassing SSRF/private-network policy, proxy/dispatcher policy, and timeout handling.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: QMD Windows `.cmd` shim resolution for quoted absolute JavaScript entrypoints and scoped `@tobilu/qmd` fallback resolution.
- Real environment tested: Windows_NT 10.0.22631, Node v24.9.0, branch `fix/qmd-newapi-real-proof`, repo `E:\sourceCode\java\my\openclaw\openclaw`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran a local Node/tsx proof script from `E:\sourceCode\java\my\openclaw\openclaw` that created a temporary Windows-style `node_modules\.bin\qmd.cmd` wrapper pointing at a quoted absolute `node_modules\@tobilu\qmd\dist\cli.js` entrypoint, then called `resolveWindowsSpawnProgram(...)`, `materializeWindowsSpawnProgram(...)`, and `resolveCliSpawnInvocation(...)`.
- Evidence after fix:
  
  ```json
  {
    "cwd": "E:\\sourceCode\\java\\my\\openclaw\\openclaw",
    "platform": "win32",
    "node": "v24.9.0",
    "shimPath": "C:\\Users\\JiaLe\\AppData\\Local\\Temp\\openclaw-qmd-proof-oOIzQo\\node_modules\\.bin\\qmd.cmd",
    "shimContainsAbsoluteJsEntrypointWithoutDp0": true,
    "packageName": "@tobilu/qmd",
    "program": {
      "command": "E:\\software\\encoding\\common\\nodeJs\\node.exe",
      "leadingArgv": [
        "C:\\Users\\JiaLe\\AppData\\Local\\Temp\\openclaw-qmd-proof-oOIzQo\\node_modules\\@tobilu\\qmd\\dist\\cli.js"
      ],
      "resolution": "node-entrypoint",
      "windowsHide": true
    },
    "invocation": {
      "command": "E:\\software\\encoding\\common\\nodeJs\\node.exe",
      "argv": [
        "C:\\Users\\JiaLe\\AppData\\Local\\Temp\\openclaw-qmd-proof-oOIzQo\\node_modules\\@tobilu\\qmd\\dist\\cli.js",
        "--version"
      ],
      "resolution": "node-entrypoint",
      "windowsHide": true
    },
    "cliInvocation": {
      "command": "E:\\software\\encoding\\common\\nodeJs\\node.exe",
      "argv": [
        "C:\\Users\\JiaLe\\AppData\\Local\\Temp\\openclaw-qmd-proof-oOIzQo\\node_modules\\@tobilu\\qmd\\dist\\cli.js",
        "--help"
      ],
      "resolution": "node-entrypoint",
      "windowsHide": true
    },
    "shellFallbackUsed": false
  }
  ```
- Observed result after fix: The quoted absolute `.cmd` shim entrypoint resolves to direct Node execution of the `@tobilu/qmd` JavaScript entrypoint, and `shellFallbackUsed` is `false`.
- What was not tested: Full live QMD memory indexing/search workflow and macOS were not tested in this proof run. Image URL guarded fetching is covered by targeted unit tests and code inspection rather than this Windows runtime proof.

## Image generation validation

Validated the OpenAI-compatible URL-response parser and guarded download path with targeted tests.

Before this fix, OpenClaw parsed only `data[].b64_json`, causing URL-only image responses to fail with no generated image assets.

After this fix:

- URL-only OpenAI-compatible image responses produce generated image assets
- `b64_json` remains preferred when present
- URL downloads go through guarded provider HTTP instead of raw `fetch`

For the OpenAI-compatible image URL path, I also ran `image_generate` locally through the configured OpenAI-compatible image provider after this patch.
Evidence after fix:
```text
Generated 1 image with openai/gpt-image-2.

Observed result after fix: URL-backed OpenAI-compatible image generation completed successfully and produced a generated image asset in the local OpenClaw setup.


## Testing

```bash
pnpm exec vitest run src/plugin-sdk/windows-spawn.test.ts
```

```bash
pnpm exec vitest run src/image-generation/image-assets.test.ts src/plugin-sdk/windows-spawn.test.ts
```

Result:

```text
2 test files passed
10 tests passed
```

```bash
pnpm exec vitest run extensions/memory-core/src/memory/qmd-manager.test.ts -t "resolves bare qmd command through the scoped QMD package fallback on Windows"
```

Result:

```text
1 test passed
```

```bash
pnpm format:check src/image-generation/image-assets.ts src/image-generation/image-assets.test.ts src/image-generation/openai-compatible-image-provider.ts extensions/openai/image-generation-provider.ts src/plugin-sdk/windows-spawn.ts src/plugin-sdk/windows-spawn.test.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory/qmd-manager.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory/qmd-manager.test.ts packages/memory-host-sdk/src/host/qmd-process.ts packages/memory-host-sdk/src/host/windows-spawn.ts
```

Result:

```text
All matched files use the correct format.
```

```bash
git diff --check
pnpm tsgo:core
pnpm tsgo:extensions
```

Result:

```text
passed
```

## Platform notes

The QMD wrapper-resolution change has been validated on Windows.

macOS was not tested. The changed QMD wrapper-resolution path is Windows-only (`platform === "win32"`), and non-Windows platforms continue to use the existing direct command path.

The OpenAI-compatible image response parsing change is platform-independent.

target.title: feat(cli-output): tool-use injection + rolling-timer indicator for claude-stream-json backends
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem.** When Claude calls a tool mid-turn on the `claude-cli` backend, the assistant text stream just pauses — often for tens of seconds on `Read` / `Bash` / `Grep` / `WebFetch`. The end user sees a frozen typing indicator and no signal about which tool is running.
- **Why it matters.** Looks like the gateway hung; users abandon turns that are still in flight. Tool calls live in `stream_event` records, never in `assistant_text` deltas, so the existing assistant-stream consumer never surfaces them.
- **What changed.** Two complementary surfaces on `createCliJsonlStreamingParser` (gated by `usesClaudeStreamJsonDialect`):
  1. **In-stream textual marker** via `onAssistantDelta` — `\n\n[HH:MM:SS] 🛠️ ToolName: detail\n` followed by a rolling `_ Ns — HH:MM:SS_` indicator that refreshes every 8s, stripped on the next `text_delta` or on turn end.
  2. **Structured `stream: "tool"` agent event** (`{phase: "start", name, args, itemId}`) via a new optional `onToolEvent` callback — consumed by `cli-runner/execute.ts` and forwarded to the agent event bus for downstream tool-tile UIs.
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary).** Gated by `usesClaudeStreamJsonDialect({backend, providerId})` — no impact on non-Claude CLI backends. Existing `onAssistantDelta` callers are unaffected. `onToolEvent` is opt-in. No tool-output / tool-result event in this PR (the result is reflected in claude's next `text_delta` and the timer-strip handles UX). Tool args sanitized via `sanitizeToolArgs` before either path.
- **AI-assisted:** yes — drafted with Claude.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed:** tool calls during a Claude turn now produce both an in-stream textual marker (with rolling 8s timer indicator) and a structured `stream: "tool"` agent event, when `backend.jsonlDialect === "claude-stream-json"` or `providerId === "claude-cli"`.
- **Real environment tested:** Windows 11 Pro 26200, Node v24.12.0, pnpm, `@anthropic-ai/claude-code` v2.1.142, Opus 4.7, live OpenClaw gateway driving the `claude-cli` backend per-turn over Telegram.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** `pnpm exec tsc --noEmit -p tsconfig.json`; live Telegram turn that invokes one or more tools (Read / Bash / Grep / WebFetch); observe the textual marker, the 8s tick refresh, and the marker stripping plus `\n\n` separator when assistant text resumes; verify the `stream: "tool"` agent event in `emitAgentEvent` logs.
- **Evidence after fix:**
  - Telegram screenshot of a live turn (rolling-timer rendered to the end-user surface): ![telegram rolling-timer](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/proof-assets/pr-82285/.proof/telegram-rolling-timer.jpg). Every `[HH:MM:SS] 🛠️ Bash: …` line in the screenshot is one `emitTool` firing in `createClaudeToolUseTracker` against `claude-cli`.
  - Typecheck clean: `pnpm exec tsc --noEmit -p tsconfig.json`.
  - The markers are an in-flight overlay delivered via `onAssistantDelta`, not persisted to `~/.openclaw/agents/main/sessions/*.jsonl` (which stores claude's structured `result`) or `~/.openclaw/logs/gateway.log` at default verbosity — Telegram is the canonical capture surface.
- **Observed result after fix:** tool calls render as `[HH:MM:SS] 🛠️ ToolName: detail` blocks in the assistant text, with the live timer ticking every 8s. When claude resumes streaming text, the timer is stripped and the text continues from a clean `\n\n` boundary. Agent-event consumers receive a structured `{phase: "start", name, args, itemId}` for each tool call.
- **What was not tested:** end-to-end against a dashboard / external listener for the `stream: "tool"` event surface (event is emitted, visible in `emitAgentEvent` logs, but no downstream UI verified beyond that); Linux/macOS execution (developed on Windows only); bundled MCP tool paths (`server_tool_use` / `mcp_tool_use` content-block types are matched in the tracker but I have not driven a live MCP-tool turn through it); focused unit test for `createClaudeToolUseTracker` is not added yet (see Regression Test Plan).

## Change Type

- [x] Feature

## Scope

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Integrations

## Linked

- Adds opt-in `onToolEvent` callback on `createCliJsonlStreamingParser` + in-stream textual marker over the existing `onAssistantDelta`.
- Overlap to coordinate: #80046 (CLI tool-event bridge). Maintainers' judgment on whether inline rolling markers ride alongside or after the structured bridge.

## Root Cause

N/A — feature PR. The condition this addresses (no tool-call UX during turns) is a property of the Claude CLI JSONL surface (tool calls live in `stream_event`, never in `assistant_text` deltas), not a regression in OpenClaw.

## Regression Test Plan

- **Target:** `src/agents/cli-output.test.ts` — add a `describe("tool-use tracker", …)` block.
- **Scenario locked in:** simulated `stream_event` sequence — `message_start` → `content_block_start (tool_use, name: "Read")` → 3× `content_block_delta (input_json_delta partial)` → `content_block_stop` — asserts the resulting `ClaudeToolEvent` carries the reassembled `args.file_path`, the right `itemId`, and that the parser's `assistantText` now includes the `[HH:MM:SS] 🛠️ Read: <path>` line.
- **Why smallest reliable guardrail:** the failure mode (regression of partial-JSON accumulation, wrong `content_block_stop` dispatch, verbose-gating drift) is fully exercisable by calling the parser's `push()` with a canned JSONL transcript — no live claude needed. Runs in ms.
- **Honest gap:** I have **not** added this test yet. Behaviour is exercised live in my local environment but a frozen-transcript unit test belongs with this PR before flipping out of draft. Flagging as a must-add before un-drafting.

## Risks and Mitigations

- **Risk:** partial-JSON accumulation in `createClaudeToolUseTracker` could drop or corrupt tool args if `content_block_stop` fires before all `input_json_delta` chunks arrive.
  - **Mitigation:** tracker holds the entry until `content_block_stop`, then parses `entry.input ?? entry.partialJson` via `parseClaudeToolArgs`. The pending unit test (`cli-output.test.ts`) locks in the contract for partial-JSON reassembly.
- **Risk:** rolling-timer `setInterval` leaks if a `content_block_stop` arrives but no subsequent `text_delta` fires (e.g. turn aborts mid-tool).
  - **Mitigation:** `clearToolKeepalive()` is also called on `result`, on `finish()`, and on the next `text_delta`. The parser closure owns the interval handle and explicitly clears it on every terminal path.
- **Risk:** tool args contain sensitive content (file paths, credentials in `WebFetch` URLs, etc.) and surface in both Telegram and agent events.
  - **Mitigation:** `sanitizeToolArgs` truncates and redacts known-sensitive keys before either emission path. Verbose gating (`shouldInjectToolInlineMarkers`) lets sessions opt out of inline markers without losing the structured event.
- **Risk:** the inline marker rides on `onAssistantDelta` — any consumer that strict-parses assistant text as Markdown could choke on the `_ Ns — HH:MM:SS_` indicator.
  - **Mitigation:** marker uses a deliberately conservative `\n\n[…] 🛠️ …\n` shape that's Markdown-safe (Telegram renders it verbatim). Consumers wanting strict text can ignore by subscribing only to the structured `stream: "tool"` event.

## Security Impact

- **New permissions/capabilities:** No.
- **Secrets/tokens handling changed:** No — same allowed-tools set; `sanitizeToolArgs` truncates args before emission.
- **New/changed network calls:** No.
- **Command/tool execution surface changed:** No — same tools claude already executes; this PR only renders that they're in flight.
- **Data access scope changed:** No — tracker reads `stream_event` records the parser already processes.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible: gated by `usesClaudeStreamJsonDialect`; non-Claude backends unaffected.
- `onToolEvent` is an additive optional callback — existing parser callers don't need to wire it up.
- No new config, no new env vars. Disable by not subscribing to the new event surface.

target.title: fix(tui): dismiss watchdog notice when response actually arrives
target.body:
## Summary

The TUI streaming watchdog adds **"This response is taking longer than expected. Send another message to continue."** to the chat log after 30 s without a delta. When a chat delta or `final` then arrives — common for runs that were slow but not actually stuck — the notice persists next to the recovered response and contradicts what the user sees.

This change tracks the notice by `runId` and dismisses it from `handleChatEvent` whenever any further chat event for that run is processed, so the message disappears the moment the response continues. The watchdog's internal cleanup (`activeChatRunId` reset, status idle, deferred history reload) is unchanged, so genuinely stuck runs still show the notice and recover identically.

## Implementation

- `src/tui/components/chat-log.ts` — new `addPendingSystem(runId, text)` / `dismissPendingSystem(runId)` pair, keyed by `runId` (mirrors the existing `pendingUsers` map at `addPendingUser` / `dropPendingUser`). `clearAll` and `dropComponentReferences` updated.
- `src/tui/tui-event-handlers.ts` — watchdog timer routes through `addPendingSystem(runId, …)`, and `handleChatEvent` calls `dismissPendingSystem(evt.runId)` after early-return checks but before `noteSessionRun`, so any delta / final / aborted / error for the run drops a stale notice.
- Tests cover the happy-path display, dismissal on a late delta, dismissal on a late final, and the chat-log component contract.

## Related

- Refs #67052 ("TUI streaming indicator stays active long after response content finishes") — same surface, adjacent UX bug; this PR addresses the user-visible notice rather than the streaming-status indicator timing, so #67052 stays open.
- Refs #69081 (closed) — the watchdog desync issue whose tool/lifecycle proof-of-life fix already shipped on `main`.
- Prior attempt #69026 by @jpruit20 raised the threshold to 120 s and suppressed the notice entirely; this PR is the narrower fix that keeps the warning useful for genuinely stuck runs. #69026 can be closed in favor of this once merged.

## Verification

- `pnpm test src/tui/tui-event-handlers.test.ts src/tui/components/chat-log.test.ts` — 61 passed (including 2 new dismissal tests + 2 new chat-log tests).
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/tui/tui-event-handlers.ts src/tui/tui-event-handlers.test.ts src/tui/components/chat-log.ts src/tui/components/chat-log.test.ts CHANGELOG.md` — clean.
- `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs src/tui/tui-event-handlers.ts src/tui/tui-event-handlers.test.ts src/tui/components/chat-log.ts src/tui/components/chat-log.test.ts` — 0 warnings, 0 errors.
- `pnpm check:changed` — exit 0 (core + core-tests lanes selected).

## Test plan

- [ ] CI green
- [ ] Manual TUI: trigger a run that exceeds 30 s without a delta, see the notice; let the run continue and confirm the notice disappears once a delta/final arrives
- [ ] Manual TUI: trigger a genuinely stuck run (kill gateway mid-run); confirm the notice still appears and persists

target.title: fix(auto-reply): deliver usage footer in message-tool-only visible reply mode
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #83071.

When `messages.groupChat.visibleReplies = "message_tool"` (e.g. Telegram forum-topic group chats), the agent sends visible replies through the message tool during the turn. The usage footer is appended to `finalPayloads` after the LLM run completes, but since `suppressDelivery` is `true` for message-tool-only mode, those payloads are never delivered to the user — the usage footer is silently dropped.

## Root Cause

In `dispatch-from-config.ts`, when `sourceReplyDeliveryMode` is `message_tool_only`:
1. `suppressAutomaticSourceDelivery = true` (source-reply-delivery-mode.ts)
2. `suppressDelivery = sendPolicyDenied || suppressAutomaticSourceDelivery` → `true`
3. The final payload delivery loop (~line 1644) skips all reply payloads including the one carrying the usage footer

The usage footer is computed and appended in `agent-runner.ts` (via `appendUsageLine`) only *after* the LLM run completes. By that point, message-tool visible replies have already been sent during tool execution, and the final payloads are discarded by the suppression guard.

## Fix

After the main reply delivery loop, when replies were suppressed due to `suppressAutomaticSourceDelivery` (not `sendPolicyDenied`) and no other delivery was attempted, extract the usage line (`Usage: ...`) from the last suppressed reply and deliver it as a standalone final payload via `sendFinalPayload`.

This mirrors the existing `deliverDespiteSourceReplySuppression` mechanism already used for runtime failure notices in message-tool-only mode.

**Guard conditions:**
- Only fires when `suppressAutomaticSourceDelivery && !sendPolicyDenied` — sendPolicy deny still wins
- Only fires when `!attemptedFinalDelivery` — avoids double-delivery when `deliverDespiteSourceReplySuppression` already delivered some payloads
- Searches backward from the last reply for a line starting with `Usage: ` (matching the exact prefix from `formatResponseUsageLine`)

## Changes

- `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.ts`: Add post-loop usage footer extraction and delivery for message-tool-only mode
- `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.test.ts`: Add regression test confirming usage footer is extracted and delivered as standalone message

## Testing

```
npx vitest run src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.test.ts -t "delivers usage footer"
# ✓ delivers usage footer as standalone message in message-tool-only mode (4379ms)
# Test Files  1 passed (1)
# Tests  1 passed | 112 skipped (113)
```

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed:** Usage footer silently dropped in Telegram forum-topic group chats configured with `visibleReplies = "message_tool"` (message-tool-only mode). The usage line was computed and appended to `finalPayloads` by `appendUsageLine()`, but never delivered because `suppressDelivery = true` skipped the entire final payload loop.

**Real environment tested:** Ubuntu 24.04 (kernel 6.17.0-22-generic), Node v22.22.2, openclaw development checkout at commit `efa7095ace` on branch `fix/usage-footer-message-tool-delivery`.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

1. Applied the patch to `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.ts`
2. Ran a standalone node verification script exercising the exact extraction logic added in the patch — replicating the runtime conditions (`suppressAutomaticSourceDelivery=true`, `sendPolicyDenied=false`, reply containing `\nUsage: ...` suffix):

```bash
$ node --experimental-strip-types /tmp/verify-usage-footer.mts
```

3. Verified the negative guard (sendPolicyDenied blocks delivery):

```bash
$ node --experimental-strip-types -e '<inline negative-case script>'
```

**Evidence after fix:**

Terminal output from the runtime verification:
```
$ node --experimental-strip-types /tmp/verify-usage-footer.mts
=== Usage Footer Extraction Verification ===
suppressAutomaticSourceDelivery: true
sendPolicyDenied: false
attemptedFinalDelivery: false
Input reply text: "Here is my answer to your question about TypeScript generics.
Usage: 1.2K in / 500 out · cache 800 cached / 200 new · est $0.02"
---
Extracted usage footer: "Usage: 1.2K in / 500 out · cache 800 cached / 200 new · est $0.02"
Would deliver via sendFinalPayload: true
✓ PASS: Usage footer correctly extracted and would be delivered
```

Negative case (sendPolicyDenied guard):
```
$ node --experimental-strip-types -e '...'
=== Negative case: sendPolicyDenied ===
sendPolicyDenied: true → usage footer NOT delivered: ✓ CORRECT
```

Supplemental — focused unit test exercising the full dispatch pipeline with mocked dispatcher confirming `sendFinalReply` is called exactly once with the extracted usage line:
```
$ npx vitest run src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.test.ts -t "delivers usage footer"
 ✓ delivers usage footer as standalone message in message-tool-only mode 17ms
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
 Tests  1 passed | 112 skipped (113)
```

**Observed result after fix:** The usage footer extraction logic correctly identifies the `\nUsage: ` boundary in suppressed reply text, slices out only the usage line (no answer text leakage), and delivers it via `sendFinalPayload`. The `sendPolicyDenied` guard correctly prevents delivery when policy blocks it. The `attemptedFinalDelivery` guard prevents double-delivery when `deliverDespiteSourceReplySuppression` already sent payloads.

**What was not tested:** Live Telegram forum-topic delivery end-to-end — requires a Telegram supergroup with forum topics enabled and `visibleReplies = "message_tool"` config. The extraction logic is deterministic string manipulation independent of transport.

---
*AI-assisted (kagura-agent)*
