target.title: feat(cli-output): tool-use injection + rolling-timer indicator for claude-stream-json backends
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem.** When Claude calls a tool mid-turn on the `claude-cli` backend, the assistant text stream just pauses — often for tens of seconds on `Read` / `Bash` / `Grep` / `WebFetch`. The end user sees a frozen typing indicator and no signal about which tool is running.
- **Why it matters.** Looks like the gateway hung; users abandon turns that are still in flight. Tool calls live in `stream_event` records, never in `assistant_text` deltas, so the existing assistant-stream consumer never surfaces them.
- **What changed.** Two complementary surfaces on `createCliJsonlStreamingParser` (gated by `usesClaudeStreamJsonDialect`):
  1. **In-stream textual marker** via `onAssistantDelta` — `\n\n[HH:MM:SS] 🛠️ ToolName: detail\n` followed by a rolling `_ Ns — HH:MM:SS_` indicator that refreshes every 8s, stripped on the next `text_delta` or on turn end.
  2. **Structured `stream: "tool"` agent event** (`{phase: "start", name, args, itemId}`) via a new optional `onToolEvent` callback — consumed by `cli-runner/execute.ts` and forwarded to the agent event bus for downstream tool-tile UIs.
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary).** Gated by `usesClaudeStreamJsonDialect({backend, providerId})` — no impact on non-Claude CLI backends. Existing `onAssistantDelta` callers are unaffected. `onToolEvent` is opt-in. No tool-output / tool-result event in this PR (the result is reflected in claude's next `text_delta` and the timer-strip handles UX). Tool args sanitized via `sanitizeToolArgs` before either path.
- **AI-assisted:** yes — drafted with Claude.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed:** tool calls during a Claude turn now produce both an in-stream textual marker (with rolling 8s timer indicator) and a structured `stream: "tool"` agent event, when `backend.jsonlDialect === "claude-stream-json"` or `providerId === "claude-cli"`.
- **Real environment tested:** Windows 11 Pro 26200, Node v24.12.0, pnpm, `@anthropic-ai/claude-code` v2.1.142, Opus 4.7, live OpenClaw gateway driving the `claude-cli` backend per-turn over Telegram.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** `pnpm exec tsc --noEmit -p tsconfig.json`; live Telegram turn that invokes one or more tools (Read / Bash / Grep / WebFetch); observe the textual marker, the 8s tick refresh, and the marker stripping plus `\n\n` separator when assistant text resumes; verify the `stream: "tool"` agent event in `emitAgentEvent` logs.
- **Evidence after fix:**
  - Telegram screenshot of a live turn (rolling-timer rendered to the end-user surface): ![telegram rolling-timer](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/proof-assets/pr-82285/.proof/telegram-rolling-timer.jpg). Every `[HH:MM:SS] 🛠️ Bash: …` line in the screenshot is one `emitTool` firing in `createClaudeToolUseTracker` against `claude-cli`.
  - Typecheck clean: `pnpm exec tsc --noEmit -p tsconfig.json`.
  - The markers are an in-flight overlay delivered via `onAssistantDelta`, not persisted to `~/.openclaw/agents/main/sessions/*.jsonl` (which stores claude's structured `result`) or `~/.openclaw/logs/gateway.log` at default verbosity — Telegram is the canonical capture surface.
- **Observed result after fix:** tool calls render as `[HH:MM:SS] 🛠️ ToolName: detail` blocks in the assistant text, with the live timer ticking every 8s. When claude resumes streaming text, the timer is stripped and the text continues from a clean `\n\n` boundary. Agent-event consumers receive a structured `{phase: "start", name, args, itemId}` for each tool call.
- **What was not tested:** end-to-end against a dashboard / external listener for the `stream: "tool"` event surface (event is emitted, visible in `emitAgentEvent` logs, but no downstream UI verified beyond that); Linux/macOS execution (developed on Windows only); bundled MCP tool paths (`server_tool_use` / `mcp_tool_use` content-block types are matched in the tracker but I have not driven a live MCP-tool turn through it); focused unit test for `createClaudeToolUseTracker` is not added yet (see Regression Test Plan).

## Change Type

- [x] Feature

## Scope

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Integrations

## Linked

- Adds opt-in `onToolEvent` callback on `createCliJsonlStreamingParser` + in-stream textual marker over the existing `onAssistantDelta`.
- Overlap to coordinate: #80046 (CLI tool-event bridge). Maintainers' judgment on whether inline rolling markers ride alongside or after the structured bridge.

## Root Cause

N/A — feature PR. The condition this addresses (no tool-call UX during turns) is a property of the Claude CLI JSONL surface (tool calls live in `stream_event`, never in `assistant_text` deltas), not a regression in OpenClaw.

## Regression Test Plan

- **Target:** `src/agents/cli-output.test.ts` — add a `describe("tool-use tracker", …)` block.
- **Scenario locked in:** simulated `stream_event` sequence — `message_start` → `content_block_start (tool_use, name: "Read")` → 3× `content_block_delta (input_json_delta partial)` → `content_block_stop` — asserts the resulting `ClaudeToolEvent` carries the reassembled `args.file_path`, the right `itemId`, and that the parser's `assistantText` now includes the `[HH:MM:SS] 🛠️ Read: <path>` line.
- **Why smallest reliable guardrail:** the failure mode (regression of partial-JSON accumulation, wrong `content_block_stop` dispatch, verbose-gating drift) is fully exercisable by calling the parser's `push()` with a canned JSONL transcript — no live claude needed. Runs in ms.
- **Honest gap:** I have **not** added this test yet. Behaviour is exercised live in my local environment but a frozen-transcript unit test belongs with this PR before flipping out of draft. Flagging as a must-add before un-drafting.

## Risks and Mitigations

- **Risk:** partial-JSON accumulation in `createClaudeToolUseTracker` could drop or corrupt tool args if `content_block_stop` fires before all `input_json_delta` chunks arrive.
  - **Mitigation:** tracker holds the entry until `content_block_stop`, then parses `entry.input ?? entry.partialJson` via `parseClaudeToolArgs`. The pending unit test (`cli-output.test.ts`) locks in the contract for partial-JSON reassembly.
- **Risk:** rolling-timer `setInterval` leaks if a `content_block_stop` arrives but no subsequent `text_delta` fires (e.g. turn aborts mid-tool).
  - **Mitigation:** `clearToolKeepalive()` is also called on `result`, on `finish()`, and on the next `text_delta`. The parser closure owns the interval handle and explicitly clears it on every terminal path.
- **Risk:** tool args contain sensitive content (file paths, credentials in `WebFetch` URLs, etc.) and surface in both Telegram and agent events.
  - **Mitigation:** `sanitizeToolArgs` truncates and redacts known-sensitive keys before either emission path. Verbose gating (`shouldInjectToolInlineMarkers`) lets sessions opt out of inline markers without losing the structured event.
- **Risk:** the inline marker rides on `onAssistantDelta` — any consumer that strict-parses assistant text as Markdown could choke on the `_ Ns — HH:MM:SS_` indicator.
  - **Mitigation:** marker uses a deliberately conservative `\n\n[…] 🛠️ …\n` shape that's Markdown-safe (Telegram renders it verbatim). Consumers wanting strict text can ignore by subscribing only to the structured `stream: "tool"` event.

## Security Impact

- **New permissions/capabilities:** No.
- **Secrets/tokens handling changed:** No — same allowed-tools set; `sanitizeToolArgs` truncates args before emission.
- **New/changed network calls:** No.
- **Command/tool execution surface changed:** No — same tools claude already executes; this PR only renders that they're in flight.
- **Data access scope changed:** No — tracker reads `stream_event` records the parser already processes.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible: gated by `usesClaudeStreamJsonDialect`; non-Claude backends unaffected.
- `onToolEvent` is an additive optional callback — existing parser callers don't need to wire it up.
- No new config, no new env vars. Disable by not subscribing to the new event surface.

target.title: feat(anthropic): claude-cli-interactive backend — stream reasoning via local TLS proxy (experimental)
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem.** OpenClaw's `claude-cli` backend invokes `claude -p --output-format stream-json` (headless). Starting **June 15, 2026** that path moves onto Anthropic's separate programmatic credit pool ([VentureBeat coverage](https://venturebeat.com/technology/anthropic-reinstates-openclaw-and-third-party-agent-usage-on-claude-subscriptions-with-a-catch)) — fixed monthly allowance, billed at API rates, doesn't roll over.
- **Why it matters.** Every OpenClaw Opus turn on a Claude subscription will drain that fixed credit post–June 15 instead of counting against the interactive subscription limits subscribers are paying for. Opus turns exhaust the credit fast.
- **What changed.** New opt-in `claude-cli-interactive` backend. Each turn spawns one fresh `bun wrapper.ts …` that boots a loopback HTTPS MITM on a 127.0.0.1 port, taps every API SSE event, re-emits the events as `claude -p --output-format stream-json` JSONL records, and kills the claude child on `end_turn`. A `tty-spoof.cjs` `NODE_OPTIONS=--require` preload keeps claude in interactive mode under piped stdio.
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary).** Existing `claude-cli` backend untouched. `agents.defaults.cliBackends.claude-cli` user overrides inherit into the interactive backend via `inheritUserConfigFrom` with `-p`-mode flags stripped. System CA store is not mutated (`NODE_EXTRA_CA_CERTS` scoped to the spawned claude child only). Non–`api.anthropic.com` CONNECT tunnels pass through unmodified. Tool-use rendering / waiting-on-tool injection lands in #82285, not here.
- **AI-assisted:** yes — drafted with Claude.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed:** `claude` running under OpenClaw in interactive (subscription) mode with full `init` / `stream_event*` / `result` JSONL reconstruction on the wrapper's stdout, sourced from the loopback MITM proxy's SSE tap.
- **Real environment tested:** Windows 11 Pro 26200, Node v24.12.0, pnpm, Bun, `@anthropic-ai/claude-code` v2.1.142 (npm-installed), Opus 4.7 (`claude-opus-4-7`), live OpenClaw gateway driving the backend per-turn over Telegram.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** `unset NODE_OPTIONS && pnpm exec vitest run extensions/anthropic/cli-backend-interactive.test.ts`; `pnpm exec tsc --noEmit -p extensions/anthropic/tsconfig.json`; set `cliBackends.claude-cli-interactive` active in user config; restart the OpenClaw gateway; send a Telegram message that triggers an Opus 4.7 turn; observe wrapper boot in stderr, SSE flow through `127.0.0.1:<port>`, JSONL arriving at OpenClaw; send a follow-up message with `--resume <sessionId>` to verify resume.
- **Evidence after fix:**
  - vitest: `Test Files 1 passed (1) / Tests 5 passed (5)` — `normalizeConfig` branches (inherited claude-cli match, direct Bun-path override, env passthrough, no-override skip).
  - Gateway log excerpt + cert listing + active-config snapshot on the [`proof-assets/pr-81851`](https://github.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/tree/proof-assets/pr-81851/.proof) branch. Embedded sample (one of three real turns in the gateway-log excerpt):

    ```
    cli exec: provider=claude-cli-interactive model=opus useResume=true session=present
    cli argv: bun <NPM>\…\wrapper.ts --effort max --verbose --setting-sources user --resume <SESSION> --allowedTools mcp__openclaw__* --model opus <prompt:N chars>
    [openclaw-proxy] certs ready: <HOME>\.openclaw\proxy-certs\ca.crt
    [openclaw-proxy] MITM proxy active on 127.0.0.1:17081 — Anthropic API traffic intercepted locally to capture thinking_delta events
    [openclaw-proxy] spawning claude binary: <NPM>\…\@anthropic-ai\claude-code\bin\claude.exe
    [openclaw-proxy] result flushed, waiting for session write before kill
    [openclaw-proxy] session file updated, killing claude
    ```
- **Observed result after fix:** per-turn lifecycle terminates cleanly — wrapper emits `init`, a continuous stream of `stream_event` records, then a single `result` on `end_turn`, sets `intentionalKill = true`, and `claude.kill()`s the child so the per-turn process tree exits 0.
- **What was not tested:** Linux/macOS (developed on Windows 11 only); bundled-MCP `claude-config-file` mode end-to-end with multiple MCP servers active (only `mcp__openclaw__*` exercised live); forced Anthropic-side `429` / `overloaded_error` mid-stream (the `api_error` synthetic event path is implemented but I did not provoke a real 429).

## Change Type

- [x] Feature

## Scope

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Integrations

## Linked

- Related: #81550 (programmatic-mode reasoning bridge — this PR is the interactive-mode equivalent on a separate backend); #82285 (tool-use injection, builds on the `stream_event` surface this PR establishes).

## Root Cause

N/A — feature PR. The lack of thinking visibility on subscription is a property of `claude -p` (headless) mode, not a regression in OpenClaw.

## Regression Test Plan

- **Target:** `extensions/anthropic/cli-backend-interactive.test.ts`.
- **Scenario locked in:** `normalizeConfig` post-merge shape across four branches — inherited claude-cli match (forces `command="bun"`, stashes binary on env), direct Bun-path override (keeps the override as the spawn command, no env stash), env passthrough preservation, no-override case (env passthrough omitted so wrapper falls back to PATH lookup of `"claude"`).
- **Why smallest reliable guardrail:** the failure mode is config-shape drift inside `normalizeConfig` — fully exercised synchronously without spawning bun or claude, runs in ms vs the minutes an e2e run would cost.

## Risks and Mitigations

- **Risk:** newly-installed user with no inherited `claude-cli` override could spawn `bun` as the claude binary if `normalizeConfig` stashed `merged.command` indiscriminately.
  - **Mitigation:** `normalizeConfig` consults `context.config.agents.defaults.cliBackends.claude-cli.command`; only stashes the env passthrough on a match. Regression test asserts the env var is `undefined` for the no-override branch.
- **Risk:** `tty-spoof.cjs` is a runtime support file (not a build output) and could be omitted from packaged installs.
  - **Mitigation:** declared in `extensions/anthropic/package.json#openclaw.build.staticAssets`; postbuild asset copier ships it alongside the wrapper.
- **Risk:** winget-installed `claude.exe` shadows the npm install via PATHEXT on Windows.
  - **Mitigation:** wrapper spawns `OPENCLAW_INTERACTIVE_CLAUDE_BINARY` directly when the parent backend resolved a binary, never falling back to a bare PATH lookup.
- **Risk:** title-gen / Haiku side-requests inject `{"title":"…"}` artifacts into the reasoning stream.
  - **Mitigation:** mitm-server classifies each `/v1/messages` request into `auxiliary` (no tools) / `compaction` (compact.ts prompt markers) / `tool_followup` (last msg has tool_result) / `normal`. Wrapper drops auxiliary entirely, streams compaction as `thinking_delta`, emits a result only on `normal` `end_turn`. Response-content fingerprint backup (`<analysis>` + Primary-Request-and-Intent + Pending-Tasks) catches anything that slipped past the request-level classifier.
- **Risk:** spilled-prompt overflow file leaks to concurrent runs via shared tempdir + `--add-dir`.
  - **Mitigation:** per-run subdir under `${tmpdir()}/openclaw-interactive-proxy/run-<32-hex-bytes>/` (0700), file 0600, `--add-dir` scoped to the per-run subdir only.
- **Risk:** local CA + leaf key persisted under user's OpenClaw config dir could be reused if an attacker gets read access.
  - **Mitigation:** trust scoped to the spawned claude child via `NODE_EXTRA_CA_CERTS` only (no system trust mutation); CA issues solely the one `api.anthropic.com` leaf the per-turn proxy needs; key files hardened to 0600 with fail-closed regeneration; maintainer security review invited.
- **Risk:** Anthropic-side non-SSE 4xx/5xx on `/v1/messages` (429, billing, overloaded) hangs the wrapper because claude doesn't self-exit on API error.
  - **Mitigation:** mitm-server emits a synthetic `api_error` event for non-SSE 4xx/5xx; wrapper surfaces a `result` with `is_error: true` and exits nonzero so the CLI runner treats the turn as failed and triggers failover.
- **Risk:** two resumes of the same Claude session race on the same transcript.
  - **Mitigation:** backend config sets `serialize: true` so the framework queues same-session runs; different sessions still run concurrently. mitm-server binds both proxy stages on port 0 so concurrent proxies can't collide on a preferred port either.

## Security Impact

- **New permissions/capabilities:** Yes — per-turn local TLS MITM on a loopback port. Scoped to the spawned claude child via `NODE_EXTRA_CA_CERTS`; system trust store untouched. Local CA + leaf key cached under user's OpenClaw config dir at 0700/0600.
- **Secrets/tokens handling changed:** No — wrapper does not read, log, or modify the Anthropic auth token; it is forwarded byte-for-byte to the re-issued upstream request.
- **New/changed network calls:** Yes — claude's outbound HTTPS to `api.anthropic.com:443` is rerouted via `HTTPS_PROXY=http://127.0.0.1:<port>` inside the child. Non–`api.anthropic.com` CONNECT tunnels (statsig, datadog, etc.) bypass the TLS terminator entirely.
- **Command/tool execution surface changed:** No — same `claude` binary, same allowed-tools (`mcp__openclaw__*`), same session semantics.
- **Data access scope changed:** No — proxy sees only the SSE bytes claude itself sees on the wire; nothing exfiltrated or aggregated outside the per-turn process.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible: existing `claude-cli` backend unchanged. Opt-in via the `claude-cli-interactive` id; no default flips.
- New optional env: `OPENCLAW_INTERACTIVE_PROXY_DEBUG=1` enables verbose wrapper stderr.
- Migration needed: no.

## Prior art

[`smithersai/claude-p`](https://github.com/smithersai/claude-p) runs `claude` without `-p` but bypasses the JSONL streaming surface entirely — works for the raw final text but loses the `stream_event` records that reasoning and tool-use rendering depend on. This PR reproduces the `-p --output-format stream-json` shape record-for-record from the SSE tap so existing OpenClaw stream consumers layer on unchanged.

target.title: fix(code-mode): surface timeout and runtime_unavailable error codes (#83389)
target.body:
Fixes #83389.

`CodeModeErrorCode` documents `"timeout"` and `"runtime_unavailable"` in `docs/reference/code-mode.md:621-641`, but two known QuickJS-WASI worker failure paths in `src/agents/code-mode.ts` fell through to `"internal_error"`:

1. **Timer-triggered terminate** (QuickJS interrupt-handler timeout, e.g. `while (true) {}`) at `runCodeModeWorker:514` — now surfaces `code: "timeout"`.
2. **`worker.on("error")` for a missing/unloadable QuickJS-WASI worker module** (`Cannot find module .../code-mode.worker.js`, `ERR_MODULE_NOT_FOUND`, `ERR_WORKER_PATH`) at line 535 — now surfaces `code: "runtime_unavailable"`.

`internal_error` stays as the fallback for genuine unexpected failures (assertion-style crashes, non-Error throws, unmatched worker error shapes).

## Changes

- `src/agents/code-mode.ts`: widen `CodeModeWorkerResult.code` union to include `"timeout" | "runtime_unavailable"`. Add `RUNTIME_UNAVAILABLE_ERROR_PATTERNS` + `isRuntimeUnavailableError` classifier. Update timer-terminate site to `code: "timeout"`. Update worker.on("error") site to classify by message. Comments reference #83389 at all three sites. Expose the classifier through `__testing` for unit-testability.
- `src/agents/code-mode.test.ts`: extend the existing `terminates hostile infinite loops outside the main event loop` test to assert `code === "timeout"`. Add 1 new regression case for `isRuntimeUnavailableError` covering 3 real Node error-message shapes + 4 negative controls.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** With code mode enabled, dispatching `while (true) {}` produced `code: "internal_error"` (despite the documented `"timeout"` code); and after removing/renaming the QuickJS-WASI worker module, dispatching any code produced `code: "internal_error"` (despite the documented `"runtime_unavailable"` code). Programmatic dispatch logic against `before_tool_call` / `after_tool_call` could not distinguish these two known failure paths from genuine internal errors.

- **Real environment tested:** Local Node 22.x. Probe at `/tmp/probe_83389.mjs` replays the `isRuntimeUnavailableError` classifier verbatim (3 positive cases from real Node error messages + 4 negative controls + 1 case-insensitive check) and documents the end-to-end mapping for both #83389 repros.

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** `node /tmp/probe_83389.mjs`

- **Evidence after fix:**

```
PASS: real "Cannot find module ..." → runtime_unavailable
PASS: real ERR_MODULE_NOT_FOUND → runtime_unavailable
PASS: ERR_WORKER_PATH → runtime_unavailable
PASS: 4 negative controls correctly NOT classified as runtime_unavailable
PASS: case-insensitive match works

ALL CASES PASS

=== End-to-end mapping ===
Repro 1 (while(true){}) → timer fires → code='timeout' (was 'internal_error')
Repro 2 (move worker.js) → worker.on('error') → "Cannot find module '/Users/x/.nvm/versions/node/v26.1.0/lib/..." → isRuntimeUnavailableError=true → code='runtime_unavailable' (was 'internal_error')
```

- **Observed result after fix:** The two #83389 repros now surface the documented codes:
  - Repro 1: `{ status: "failed", error: "code mode worker timeout exceeded", code: "timeout" }`
  - Repro 2: `{ status: "failed", error: "Cannot find module .../code-mode.worker.js", code: "runtime_unavailable" }`
  
  Genuine internal failures (assertion failures, unmatched error shapes, non-zero worker exit) continue to surface `"internal_error"` — this is the negative-control covered by the new test (4 example messages).

- **What was not tested:** End-to-end exec against a real running gateway with the worker file actually removed. The classifier-level unit test covers the public contract that the integration relies on; the timer-terminate path is exercised by the existing infinite-loop test that I extended; the issue's repro shape (`error.message` from a Node worker construction failure) is captured by the test's positive cases.

## Audit (per CLAUDE rules)

- **Existing-helper check:** Reuses `errorMessage` for message extraction. The new `isRuntimeUnavailableError` is small (1 function + 3 patterns) and contained in this file — no broader classifier exists for "Node worker module-not-found" patterns. PASS
- **Shared-helper caller check:** `CodeModeWorkerResult` is a file-local type (no `export`); `runCodeModeWorker` is file-local. Widening the `code` union is additive — the downstream `runExec` spreads `...result` and the call sites that consume `result.code` already accept arbitrary strings via the documented `CodeModeErrorCode` public union. PASS
- **Broader-fix rival scan + recent-merge audit (per the lesson added last cycle):** `git log --oneline -10 -- src/agents/code-mode.ts` shows no recent merges. `gh search prs --state open` empty for `CodeModeErrorCode` / `code-mode timeout runtime_unavailable`. PASS

target.title: fix(barnacle): resync candidate labels on ready_for_review, remove stale labels
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Barnacle auto-response doesn't resync candidate labels when a draft PR is marked `ready_for_review`, leaving stale candidate labels (e.g. `triage: blank-template`) on PRs with meaningful bodies.
- Why it matters: Draft PRs auto-labeled with `triage: blank-template` keep that label after the author fills in the template and clicks "Ready for review", confusing triage workflow.
- What changed: Added `ready_for_review` to the auto-response workflow trigger and script allowlist; rewrote `applyPullRequestCandidateLabels` to compute and remove stale candidate labels while preserving suppression/privileged-author guards.
- What did NOT change: No changes to existing candidate label classification rules, maintainer/OWNER skip logic, or `bad-barnacle` suppression.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] CI/CD / infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #77343
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: Three gaps — (1) `.github/workflows/auto-response.yml` did not include `ready_for_review` in `pull_request_target` types, (2) `isPrCandidateEvent` allowlist in `barnacle-auto-response.mjs` did not include `ready_for_review`, and (3) `applyPullRequestCandidateLabels` was add-only (never removed stale labels).
- Missing detection / guardrail: No test for the `ready_for_review` → stale label removal path.
- Contributing context (if known): N/A

## Real behavior proof

Not applicable — this PR modifies only GitHub Actions workflow automation (`.github/workflows/auto-response.yml`).
No product code, CLI, gateway, plugin, or UI surface is touched.
Barnacle + changelog unit tests cover the changed logic (30/30 + 15/15 passed).

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
- Target test or file: `test/scripts/barnacle-auto-response.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: `ready_for_review` action removes stale candidate labels when PR body becomes meaningful
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: Directly exercises `applyPullRequestCandidateLabels` return value + `removeLabels` call path
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): None — this was a new coverage gap
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

When a maintainer-owned PR transitions from draft to `ready_for_review` (or is edited), Barnacle recomputes candidate labels and removes any stale Barnacle-owned candidate labels (e.g. `triage: blank-template`) that no longer match the PR body/files.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[PR ready_for_review] -> [add missing labels only] -> [stale labels persist]

After:
[PR ready_for_review] -> [recompute labels] -> [add missing + remove stale] -> [labels match body]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No (existing GitHub API calls)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: any (GitHub Actions)
- Runtime/container: Node 24 (GitHub Actions)
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel: GitHub auto-response workflow

### Steps

1. Open a draft PR with a blank issue template body
2. Barnacle auto-labels it with `triage: blank-template`
3. Edit body to fill in the template (Problem/Why/What)
4. Click "Ready for review"

### Expected

- `triage: blank-template` is removed; correct candidate labels applied

### Actual

- `triage: blank-template` persisted (before fix)

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: `ready_for_review` with meaningful body removes stale labels; `ready_for_review` recomputes candidate labels; `bad-barnacle` suppresses removal; maintainer-authored PRs not mutated
- Edge cases checked: `bad-barnacle` suppression, maintainer/OWNER/COLLABORATOR skip, mixed candidate label transitions
- What you did **not** verify: Live GitHub workflow run (requires merge to test end-to-end)

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes`)
- Config/env changes? (`No`)
- Migration needed? (`No`)

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: Stale label removal could incorrectly strip a label if `classifyPullRequestCandidateLabels` has a bug.
  - Mitigation: Removal only targets Barnacle-owned candidate labels (`candidateLabelValues`); `bad-barnacle` and privileged-author skips preserved; full test suite covers all paths.

target.title: [codex] Fix Anthropic Vertex npm audit regression
target.body:
## Summary

- remove the `@anthropic-ai/vertex-sdk` runtime dependency from the shipped OpenClaw package
- replace it with a small local Anthropic Vertex client built on the existing `@anthropic-ai/sdk` and `google-auth-library@10.6.2`
- update the lockfile so the npm package no longer pulls `google-auth-library@9 -> gaxios@6 -> uuid@9`

## Why

`openclaw@2026.5.2-beta.1` introduced a clean npm audit regression for consumers: `@anthropic-ai/vertex-sdk@0.16.0` depends on `google-auth-library@^9.4.2`, which resolves to `gaxios@6.7.1` and `uuid@9.0.1`. npm ignores dependency package `overrides`, so the published OpenClaw package still audited with two moderate findings even though the pnpm workspace override resolved `uuid` internally.

## Validation

- `pnpm tsgo:extensions`
- `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/anthropic-vertex`
- `pnpm exec oxlint extensions/anthropic-vertex/stream-runtime.ts`
- `pnpm audit --prod --json` reports 0 vulnerabilities
- packed this branch with `npm pack`, installed the tarball into a fresh npm project, and verified `npm audit --omit=dev --json` reports 0 vulnerabilities
- tarball install dependency tree no longer contains `@anthropic-ai/vertex-sdk`, `google-auth-library@9`, `gaxios@6`, or `uuid@9`

target.title: fix(memory-core): use CJK-aware tokenizer for dreaming dedupe (#80613)
target.body:
## Summary

The dreaming-phases dedupe path's local `tokenizeSnippet` split on `/[^a-z0-9]+/i`, producing empty token sets for pure-CJK snippets and dropping all CJK content for mixed snippets. That had two failure modes on current main:

1. Two close paraphrases of the same Chinese fact tokenized to empty sets, fell back to exact-string match, returned similarity `0`, and ended up as duplicate candidates in MEMORY.md.
2. Two semantically distinct CJK snippets that happened to share ASCII tokens (e.g. `Plan` + `exRule`) returned similarity `1.0`, so the dedupe path silently dropped one of the two distinct memories.

The memory MMR layer at `extensions/memory-core/src/memory/mmr.ts` already has a CJK-aware tokenizer (unigrams + adjacent bigrams + ASCII alphanumerics). This PR extracts it into `extensions/memory-core/src/memory/tokenize.ts` and routes the dreaming dedupe path through the same helper via `textSimilarity`. `mmr.ts` re-exports `tokenize` / `jaccardSimilarity` / `textSimilarity` so existing imports (including `mmr.test.ts`) continue to work without churn.

## Root Cause

- `extensions/memory-core/src/dreaming-phases.ts:1347` (before): `function tokenizeSnippet(snippet) { return new Set(snippet.toLowerCase().split(/[^a-z0-9]+/i).map(t => t.trim()).filter(Boolean)); }`. CJK characters fall outside `[a-z0-9]`, so they are split into delimiters and dropped.
- `extensions/memory-core/src/dreaming-phases.ts:1357` (before): `function jaccardSimilarity(left, right)` calls the broken tokenizer and, when either token set is empty, falls back to `left.trim().toLowerCase() === right.trim().toLowerCase() ? 1 : 0`. That makes close-but-not-identical CJK pairs return 0 (missed dedup) and identical-ASCII pairs return 1 (spurious dedup, drops distinct content).
- Execution path: `dedupeEntries({ entries, threshold })` (`dreaming-phases.ts:1373`) → `jaccardSimilarity(candidate.snippet, entry.snippet) >= threshold` → either misses CJK duplicates or wrongly merges distinct CJK candidates → light dreaming output → `MEMORY.md` accumulates duplicate or loses unique entries.
- clawsweeper's review on #80613 directly endorses this fix shape: *"Keep the issue open and fix the memory-core pipeline with shared CJK-aware tokenization plus a promotion sanitizer that never appends managed dreaming block text to MEMORY.md."* and *"the narrow maintainable fix is in memory-core: reuse or extract the existing CJK-aware tokenizer for dreaming dedupe."*

## Changes

- `extensions/memory-core/src/memory/tokenize.ts` (NEW): extract the CJK-aware `tokenize`, `jaccardSimilarity`, and `textSimilarity` helpers from `mmr.ts` into a shared module so both consumers route through one source of truth.
- `extensions/memory-core/src/memory/mmr.ts`: delete the in-file `CJK_RE`, `tokenize`, `jaccardSimilarity`, `textSimilarity` bodies; import them from `./tokenize.js` and re-export verbatim so existing imports (`mmr.test.ts` etc.) keep working.
- `extensions/memory-core/src/dreaming-phases.ts`: delete the ASCII-only `tokenizeSnippet` and local `jaccardSimilarity`. Replace the single call site in `dedupeEntries` with `snippetSimilarity` (aliased from `textSimilarity` in `./memory/tokenize.js`). Expose `dedupeEntries` via `__testing` for the regression test.
- `extensions/memory-core/src/dreaming-phases.test.ts`: add a `dedupeEntries — CJK-aware snippet similarity (#80613)` describe with 4 colocated regression cases: pure-CJK dedup, mixed-CJK kept distinct, English paraphrase unchanged, unrelated short snippets stay separate.

Net diff: **+86 / -103 LOC across 4 files** (1 new + 3 modified) — a reduction from removing the duplicate ASCII-only tokenizer.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Dreaming dedupe on CJK content is broken on current main. (1) Two close Chinese paraphrases (`教训：配置中实验开关字段是叫做规则` and `教训：配置里实验开关的字段叫做规则`) both tokenize to empty sets, fall through to exact-match, return similarity `0`, and both reach MEMORY.md instead of one merging into the other. (2) Two distinct CJK snippets that share ASCII tokens (`Plan 实验开关字段叫做 exRule` vs `Plan 整个产品体系彻底重构 exRule`) return similarity `1.0`, so the dedupe pass silently drops one of two semantically different memories. Issue: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/80613
- **Real environment tested**: Local OpenClaw checkout at `../openclaw-80613` on Windows 11 + Node 22.14. The before-fix smoke script reads `dreaming-phases.ts` from current `upstream/main`, extracts the production `tokenizeSnippet` + `jaccardSimilarity` bytes verbatim, and runs them against the issue's CJK scenarios. The after-fix smoke script reads the patched `extensions/memory-core/src/memory/tokenize.ts` from this PR head, prints its SHA-256, and runs the shared `tokenize` / `textSimilarity` against the same scenarios. Both scripts run via `node --experimental-strip-types` — the production function bytes drive the assertions, no mocks. PR head: `c497966ee6d12659dc750a1819969d38817f53d1`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: `git checkout fix/dreaming-cjk-tokenizer && node --experimental-strip-types ./smoke-verify-80613.mts`, where `smoke-verify-80613.mts` reads the patched `tokenize.ts` from `extensions/memory-core/src/memory/tokenize.ts`, hashes it, then runs the CJK-aware `tokenize` and `textSimilarity` against the same four scenarios used in the colocated regression test (pure-CJK paraphrase, mixed-CJK distinct, English paraphrase control, unrelated short).
- **Evidence after fix**: Terminal output captured locally on PR head `c497966ee6` (Windows 11 + Node 22.14, the patched `extensions/memory-core/src/memory/tokenize.ts` exercised via `node --experimental-strip-types` against verbatim source bytes; tokenize.ts SHA-256 `9cd5a672bb1866c2db2384af578a8efd12d0009a69553b6d7a84cc6ee048596b`).

~~~text
$ node --experimental-strip-types ./smoke-verify-80613.mts
=== Patched tokenize.ts SHA-256 (verbatim from worktree) ===
source bytes: 2819 (includes openclaw/plugin-sdk import line)
SHA-256 of source slice (with import line): 9cd5a672bb1866c2db2384af578a8efd12d0009a69553b6d7a84cc6ee048596b

=== Bug 2 reproduction: failure modes from issue #80613 ===
Pure-CJK A: "教训：配置中实验开关字段是叫做规则"
Pure-CJK B (similar): "教训：配置里实验开关的字段叫做规则"
tokenize(A).size = 30 first 8 tokens: [
  '教训', '配置',
  '置中', '中实',
  '实验', '验开',
  '开关', '关字'
]
tokenize(B).size = 30 first 8 tokens: [
  '教训', '配置',
  '置里', '里实',
  '实验', '验开',
  '开关', '关的'
]
textSimilarity(A, B) = 0.622 (was 0 with ASCII-only tokenizer; threshold 0.5 dedup now succeeds)

Mixed A (truth): "Plan 实验开关字段叫做 exRule"
Mixed B (unrelated): "Plan 整个产品体系彻底重构 exRule"
tokenize(A) = [
  'plan', 'exrule', '实验',
  '验开', '开关',   '关字',
  '字段', '段叫',   '叫做',
  '实',   '验',     '开',
  '关',   '字',     '段',
  '叫',   '做'
]
tokenize(B) = [
  'plan', 'exrule', '整个',
  '个产', '产品',   '品体',
  '体系', '系彻',   '彻底',
  '底重', '重构',   '整',
  '个',   '产',     '品',
  '体',   '系',     '彻',
  '底',   '重',     '构'
]
textSimilarity(A, B) = 0.056 (was 1.0 with ASCII-only tokenizer; threshold 0.7 dedup now correctly keeps both)

English A: "Plan config experiment toggle field is named exRule"
English B (paraphrase): "Plan configuration uses experiment toggle field named exRule"
textSimilarity(en1, en2) = 0.600 (must stay > 0.4 — Latin-script behavior unchanged)

textSimilarity('weather: sunny', 'deploy: blocked') = 0.000 (must stay < 0.3 — no over-collapse)

=== Pass/fail summary ===
[CJK paraphrase dedups]  textSimilarity = 0.622 PASS (was 0, dedup now succeeds)
[Mixed CJK kept distinct] textSimilarity = 0.056 PASS (was 1.0, two distinct facts now kept)
[English paraphrase OK]   textSimilarity = 0.600 PASS (Latin behavior unchanged)
[Unrelated short stays]   textSimilarity = 0.000 PASS (no over-collapse)
~~~

- **Observed result after fix**: Pure-CJK paraphrase similarity went from `0.000` (was missed by ASCII-only tokenizer falling through to exact-match) to **0.622** — above the typical `0.5–0.6` dedupe threshold, so the second copy is now correctly merged. The mixed-CJK distinct pair went from `1.000` (spurious merge that silently dropped one of two distinct memories) to **0.056** — well below the `0.7` threshold, so both semantically different snippets are kept. The English paraphrase control stayed at `0.600` (Latin-script behavior unchanged). Unrelated short snippets stayed at `0.000` (no over-collapse). The patched `tokenize.ts` is pinned by SHA-256 `9cd5a672bb1866c2db2384af578a8efd12d0009a69553b6d7a84cc6ee048596b`, so the proof is bound to the exact source bytes this PR ships.
- **What was not tested**: A full end-to-end light-dreaming sweep against a real CJK workspace was not run on the contributor machine (would need a populated `~/.openclaw/workspace` with Chinese daily memory files and a configured agent). The fix is a pure-function tokenizer change at the `dedupeEntries` boundary; the downstream snippet-promotion path (`short-term-promotion.ts`) consumes the returned `ShortTermRecallEntry[]` verbatim, so the only behavioral change is the now-correct dedupe arithmetic. Bug 1 from the same issue (managed dreaming block content leaking into promoted MEMORY.md entries via `buildDailySnippetChunks`/`stripManagedDailyDreamingLines` boundary mismatch) is **intentionally out of scope** here — clawsweeper flagged it as not high-confidence-reproducible from source alone (`"the raw managed-block leak has a credible source path through promotion rehydration, but still needs a focused regression to pin the exact current-main fixture"`), and the right shape is a separate promotion-path PR with a targeted fixture. Maintainers may apply `proof: override` if a live CJK workspace recording is required.

## Test

- `pnpm test extensions/memory-core/src/dreaming-phases.test.ts` — new `dedupeEntries — CJK-aware snippet similarity (#80613)` describe with 4 cases (CJK paraphrase dedup / mixed-CJK kept distinct / English paraphrase control / unrelated short kept separate); all existing cases continue to pass.
- `pnpm test extensions/memory-core/src/memory/mmr.test.ts` — unchanged; the re-exports of `tokenize` / `jaccardSimilarity` / `textSimilarity` from `mmr.ts` preserve the existing import surface so this test stays green.
- `pnpm tsgo` against the touched files — clean (LSP diagnostics on `tokenize.ts`, `mmr.ts`, `dreaming-phases.ts`, `dreaming-phases.test.ts` show only pre-existing environment-only `openclaw/plugin-sdk/*` resolution warnings; no new TypeScript errors introduced).

## Notes

- Scope: Bug 2 from issue #80613 only (the CJK dedupe path that clawsweeper marked as source-reproducible). Bug 1 (managed-block leak into MEMORY.md) is a separate fixture problem that clawsweeper said "still needs a focused regression to pin the exact current-main fixture" — it should land in a separate promotion-path PR.
- Compatibility: `mmr.ts` re-exports `tokenize` / `jaccardSimilarity` / `textSimilarity` so every existing call site (including `mmr.test.ts`) keeps working without an import-path change. The CJK-aware tokenizer is byte-for-byte the same algorithm `mmr.ts` already shipped, so MMR behavior is unchanged.
- This follows the conservative direction in clawsweeper's review on #80613: *"the narrow maintainable fix is in memory-core: reuse or extract the existing CJK-aware tokenizer for dreaming dedupe... rather than adding a new config or policy knob."*

Closes #80613

target.title: fix(barnacle): resync candidate labels on ready_for_review, remove stale labels
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Barnacle auto-response doesn't resync candidate labels when a draft PR is marked `ready_for_review`, leaving stale candidate labels (e.g. `triage: blank-template`) on PRs with meaningful bodies.
- Why it matters: Draft PRs auto-labeled with `triage: blank-template` keep that label after the author fills in the template and clicks "Ready for review", confusing triage workflow.
- What changed: Added `ready_for_review` to the auto-response workflow trigger and script allowlist; rewrote `applyPullRequestCandidateLabels` to compute and remove stale candidate labels while preserving suppression/privileged-author guards.
- What did NOT change: No changes to existing candidate label classification rules, maintainer/OWNER skip logic, or `bad-barnacle` suppression.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] CI/CD / infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #77343
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: Three gaps — (1) `.github/workflows/auto-response.yml` did not include `ready_for_review` in `pull_request_target` types, (2) `isPrCandidateEvent` allowlist in `barnacle-auto-response.mjs` did not include `ready_for_review`, and (3) `applyPullRequestCandidateLabels` was add-only (never removed stale labels).
- Missing detection / guardrail: No test for the `ready_for_review` → stale label removal path.
- Contributing context (if known): N/A

## Real behavior proof

Not applicable — this PR modifies only GitHub Actions workflow automation (`.github/workflows/auto-response.yml`).
No product code, CLI, gateway, plugin, or UI surface is touched.
Barnacle + changelog unit tests cover the changed logic (30/30 + 15/15 passed).

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
- Target test or file: `test/scripts/barnacle-auto-response.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: `ready_for_review` action removes stale candidate labels when PR body becomes meaningful
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: Directly exercises `applyPullRequestCandidateLabels` return value + `removeLabels` call path
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): None — this was a new coverage gap
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

When a maintainer-owned PR transitions from draft to `ready_for_review` (or is edited), Barnacle recomputes candidate labels and removes any stale Barnacle-owned candidate labels (e.g. `triage: blank-template`) that no longer match the PR body/files.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[PR ready_for_review] -> [add missing labels only] -> [stale labels persist]

After:
[PR ready_for_review] -> [recompute labels] -> [add missing + remove stale] -> [labels match body]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No (existing GitHub API calls)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: any (GitHub Actions)
- Runtime/container: Node 24 (GitHub Actions)
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel: GitHub auto-response workflow

### Steps

1. Open a draft PR with a blank issue template body
2. Barnacle auto-labels it with `triage: blank-template`
3. Edit body to fill in the template (Problem/Why/What)
4. Click "Ready for review"

### Expected

- `triage: blank-template` is removed; correct candidate labels applied

### Actual

- `triage: blank-template` persisted (before fix)

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: `ready_for_review` with meaningful body removes stale labels; `ready_for_review` recomputes candidate labels; `bad-barnacle` suppresses removal; maintainer-authored PRs not mutated
- Edge cases checked: `bad-barnacle` suppression, maintainer/OWNER/COLLABORATOR skip, mixed candidate label transitions
- What you did **not** verify: Live GitHub workflow run (requires merge to test end-to-end)

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes`)
- Config/env changes? (`No`)
- Migration needed? (`No`)

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: Stale label removal could incorrectly strip a label if `classifyPullRequestCandidateLabels` has a bug.
  - Mitigation: Removal only targets Barnacle-owned candidate labels (`candidateLabelValues`); `bad-barnacle` and privileged-author skips preserved; full test suite covers all paths.

target.title: [codex] Fix Anthropic Vertex npm audit regression
target.body:
## Summary

- remove the `@anthropic-ai/vertex-sdk` runtime dependency from the shipped OpenClaw package
- replace it with a small local Anthropic Vertex client built on the existing `@anthropic-ai/sdk` and `google-auth-library@10.6.2`
- update the lockfile so the npm package no longer pulls `google-auth-library@9 -> gaxios@6 -> uuid@9`

## Why

`openclaw@2026.5.2-beta.1` introduced a clean npm audit regression for consumers: `@anthropic-ai/vertex-sdk@0.16.0` depends on `google-auth-library@^9.4.2`, which resolves to `gaxios@6.7.1` and `uuid@9.0.1`. npm ignores dependency package `overrides`, so the published OpenClaw package still audited with two moderate findings even though the pnpm workspace override resolved `uuid` internally.

## Validation

- `pnpm tsgo:extensions`
- `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/anthropic-vertex`
- `pnpm exec oxlint extensions/anthropic-vertex/stream-runtime.ts`
- `pnpm audit --prod --json` reports 0 vulnerabilities
- packed this branch with `npm pack`, installed the tarball into a fresh npm project, and verified `npm audit --omit=dev --json` reports 0 vulnerabilities
- tarball install dependency tree no longer contains `@anthropic-ai/vertex-sdk`, `google-auth-library@9`, `gaxios@6`, or `uuid@9`

target.title: fix(feishu): use union_id as fallback for @mention detection in multi-bot groups
target.body:
## Problem

Fixes #40768

When multiple Feishu bots share the same group, @mentioning a specific bot is not recognized. The bot logs `message in group ... did not mention bot` even though it's correctly mentioned.

## Root Cause

Feishu `open_id` is **app-scoped** — the same bot has a different `open_id` depending on which app's token is used to query it. Confirmed by querying the same message with two different bot tokens:

```
# Same @mention, different app contexts:
writer token → mention open_id: ou_4ede388c79638a9057a432c3e3832f91
tron token   → mention open_id: ou_04dc13327d491d9e36da57826a4131f0
```

WebSocket push events deliver mention `open_id`s in a specific app context that may not match the receiving bot's own `open_id`, causing `checkBotMentioned()` to always return `false`.

## Fix

`union_id` is globally unique across all apps for the same entity — it doesn't vary by app context. This PR:

1. Fetches `union_id` alongside `open_id` from `bot/v3/info` at startup
2. Stores it in a new `botUnionIds` map in monitor state
3. Passes it through to `checkBotMentioned()` as a fallback
4. Matches mentions by `union_id` when `open_id` doesn't match

The `open_id` match is preserved as the primary check; `union_id` is a fallback, so existing behavior is unchanged for single-bot setups.

## Files Changed

- `types.ts`: add `botUnionId` to `FeishuProbeResult`
- `probe.ts`: extract `union_id` from `bot/v3/info` response
- `monitor.startup.ts`: propagate `botUnionId` through `FeishuMonitorBotIdentity`
- `monitor.state.ts`: add `botUnionIds` map with proper clear/delete
- `monitor.account.ts`: store/clear `botUnionId`, pass to `handleFeishuMessage`
- `bot.ts`: accept `botUnionId` in `checkBotMentioned` and `parseFeishuMessageEvent`, match by `union_id` when `open_id` mismatches
